proposals.title: CorgiPile-Async: Asynchronous Decentralized Training of Large Models via Shuffled, Stale-Gradient-Aware Optimization
proposals.motivation: Synchronous dense network training, as in standard data-parallel SGD, is a fundamental bottleneck for scaling large model training. It requires perfect global synchronization, leading to severe straggler problems and underutilization of heterogeneous hardware (e.g., mixing GPUs of different generations). Pre-2026, while parameter-server and decentralized SGD exist for simpler models, they struggle with the massive communication volume and complex optimization dynamics of modern dense architectures like LLMs.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by integrating a dynamically shuffled data pipeline (CorgiPile) with a novel decentralized, asynchronous communication protocol and a convergence-guaranteed optimizer that accounts for gradient staleness, we can train large dense models to full accuracy without synchronous barriers, significantly improving hardware utilization and training throughput.
proposals.method: We will design a fully decentralized training system where each worker holds a complete model replica but exchanges updates only with a small, random subset of peers per step, eliminating the global all-reduce. We will integrate the CorgiPile data shuffling strategy to ensure global data consistency despite asynchrony. The core algorithmic challenge will be developing a new optimization method, building upon pre-2026 work on delayed SGD convergence, that adapts learning rates based on measured gradient staleness distributions to preserve final model quality. We will implement and evaluate this system using modified PyTorch distributed backends.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a practical system for asynchronous dense network training that demonstrably converges for modern architectures, 2) a new optimization theory and algorithm for stale gradients in a decentralized setting, and 3) an open-source implementation showing throughput gains on heterogeneous clusters, directly addressing scalability limits in large-scale learning.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the holistic co-design of a high-throughput data shuffling pipeline (CorgiPile), a peer-to-peer communication layer, and a theoretically grounded stale-gradient optimizer specifically for large, dense models, moving beyond synchronous paradigms or asynchronous methods designed for simpler, sparse workloads.

proposals.title: CorgiPile-Single: In-Memory, Checkpointed Training of Trillion-Parameter Models on Commodity Hardware
proposals.motivation: The dominant paradigm for training massive language models requires scaling to thousands of specialized accelerators, creating immense financial and environmental barriers to research and deployment. This centralizes AI advancement in the hands of a few large corporations and limits widespread innovation. A method to train state-of-the-art models on a single machine or small cluster would democratize access and radically alter the economics of AI development.
proposals.hypothesis: By combining aggressive activation checkpointing, novel gradient accumulation strategies, and a memory-centric data shuffling system, we can train models whose parameter count far exceeds the available GPU memory, achieving convergence rates comparable to large-scale distributed training but on a single node.
proposals.method: We will extend the CorgiPile data management system to orchestrate ultra-efficient, in-memory training loops. The core technique is a full-model, layer-wise checkpointing scheme integrated with asynchronous, pipelined gradient computation, minimizing the memory footprint of activations. We will develop a 'convergence-aware shuffle' algorithm that prioritizes data diversity within memory constraints, and rigorously analyze the trade-off between recomputation cost and statistical efficiency. The system will be auto-differentiated to support arbitrary model architectures.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver an open-source system capable of training trillion-parameter models on hardware with only tens of GB of memory, validated on established benchmarks. It will provide a formal analysis of the convergence rate under heavy memory constraints, contributing a new theoretical framework for single-node massive model training.
proposals.novelty: While model parallelism and checkpointing exist, this approach inverts the problem: instead of scaling hardware to the model, we systematically compress the training process's memory demand to fit available hardware, co-designing the data pipeline, checkpointing, and optimization steps into a single, holistic system.

proposals.title: CorgiPile-Embed: Memory-Efficient Embedding Training via Dynamic, Convergence-Aware Caching and Shuffling
proposals.motivation: Large-scale recommendation and language models rely on colossal embedding tables that can consume terabytes of memory, becoming the primary bottleneck for training and deployment. Pre-2026, methods like model parallelism or hashing introduce communication overhead or accuracy loss. A holistic, system-aware optimization is needed to manage these tables efficiently on limited GPU resources without compromising model quality.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by modeling embedding access patterns during distributed training and integrating this with convergence rate analysis, we can design a dynamic caching and data shuffling strategy that drastically reduces memory footprint while maintaining statistical efficiency and final model accuracy.
proposals.method: We will extend the CorgiPile data management system to profile and predict hot/cold embedding vectors across training iterations. Using auto-differentiation of relational computations, we will build a lightweight model that predicts access patterns based on input batch features. This model will drive a dynamic caching policy, swapping embeddings between GPU HBM and CPU memory. We will co-design this with a distributed, full-batch-aware data shuffle to optimize the convergence rate, ensuring cached embeddings align with the training trajectory.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, open-source system for memory-efficient embedding layer training, directly applicable to very large scale machine learning. It will provide a principled framework for trading off memory, communication, and statistical convergence, backed by rigorous analysis and holistic evaluation on real-world recommendation and NLP tasks.
proposals.novelty: Unlike static caching or pure hashing techniques, our approach is the first to jointly optimize memory management with the convergence dynamics of the training algorithm itself, creating a feedback loop between the system's caching policy and the learning process's statistical properties.

proposals.title: AutoSync: Automated Hyperparameter Tuning for Hybrid Synchronous-Asynchronous Distributed Training
proposals.motivation: Hybrid training strategies, which blend synchronous and asynchronous parameter updates, promise to mitigate the straggler problem and improve hardware utilization in large-scale distributed learning. However, their performance is critically sensitive to hard-to-tune hyperparameters like synchronization frequency, learning rates, and staleness thresholds. Manual tuning is infeasible at scale, creating a major barrier to the adoption of these efficient methods for training modern large models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a Bayesian optimization framework, enhanced with low-fidelity proxy tasks derived from early training dynamics and model architecture, can efficiently and automatically discover near-optimal synchronization and learning parameters for hybrid training regimes.
proposals.method: We will develop AutoSync, a system that integrates with distributed training frameworks like PyTorch. It will use a multi-fidelity Bayesian optimization (MF-BO) search, where the objective is final validation loss. Low-fidelity evaluations will be performed using a subset of data (CorgiPile-style) and a scaled-down proxy model. The search space will include synchronization interval, learning rate schedules, and gradient staleness compensation factors. The system will leverage gradient-based hyperparameter tuning ideas, treating the synchronization policy as a differentiable program within a relaxed optimization formulation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical, open-source system for automating hybrid training configuration, reducing the need for expert intuition. It will deliver a systematic analysis of the interaction between synchronization parameters and model convergence rates in heterogeneous clusters, offering new insights for distributed systems design.
proposals.novelty: While hyperparameter tuning for static distributed setups exists, automating the policy for dynamic, hybrid synchronization strategies—where the optimal point balances statistical efficiency and system throughput—is an unexplored and non-trivial co-design problem. Our novel integration of multi-fidelity BO with architectural proxies for this domain is unique.

proposals.title: CorgiPile-Lite: A Resource-Efficient Hybrid Training Framework for Small-Scale LLM Development
proposals.motivation: Hybrid training algorithms, which combine different data, model, and pipeline parallelism strategies, are crucial for training large language models (LLMs) but are designed for massive GPU clusters, creating a high barrier to entry for academic labs and startups. Pre-2026, efficient small-scale training systems are needed to democratize LLM research and enable rapid prototyping of novel architectures and training techniques without exorbitant computational resources.
proposals.hypothesis: By co-designing a streamlined hybrid training scheduler with a compressed, semantically rich data curriculum (a 'small CorgiPile'), we can achieve near-optimal convergence rates and final model quality on a single multi-GPU node or small cluster, dramatically reducing the cost of experimental LLM training cycles.
proposals.method: We will develop a lightweight hybrid parallel planner that dynamically selects between ZeRO-3, tensor, and pipeline parallelism based on real-time memory and communication profiling on a single node. This will be integrated with a 'CorgiPile-Lite' dataset, constructed via data distillation and importance sampling from larger corpora to maximize learning signal per token. The training loop will employ an efficient deterministic shuffle and gradient accumulation scheme to maintain statistical efficiency while minimizing inter-GPU synchronization overhead.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver an open-source, production-ready training system that enables the development of billion-parameter class models on limited hardware. It will provide a rigorous analysis of the convergence-rate trade-offs in scaled-down hybrid parallelism and establish best practices for data curation in resource-constrained environments.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work focused on extreme scale, this proposal explicitly optimizes the hybrid training *algorithm* and *data pipeline* for the small-scale regime, introducing the concept of a performance-model-guided micro-parallel planner and a quality-preserving compressed data pile for efficient learning.

proposals.title: Dual: A Co-Designed Architecture for Ultra-Low-Latency Trillion-Parameter Inference
proposals.motivation: The deployment of trillion-parameter models for real-time applications is bottlenecked by inference latency, as current systems are primarily optimized for distributed training throughput. Pre-2026 scaling trends show model size outpacing memory and interconnect bandwidth, making low-latency serving a critical unsolved challenge for interactive AI.
proposals.hypothesis: By co-designing the model architecture, parallelization strategy, and memory hierarchy specifically for the inference workload, we can achieve order-of-magnitude latency reductions for trillion-parameter models compared to systems adapted from training frameworks.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel hybrid parallelization scheme combining expert-choice MoE with a latency-optimal model-parallel layout, minimizing cross-device communication during the autoregressive forward pass. The system will integrate a just-in-time kernel compiler for fused attention and MoE operations, and a predictive scheduling algorithm that exploits the sequential nature of inference to prefetch parameters based on speculative execution graphs derived from recent queries.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver an open-source inference system, Dual, demonstrating sub-100ms latency for trillion-parameter models on commodity GPU clusters. It will provide a new benchmark for inference efficiency and a design framework for co-optimizing model architecture and serving infrastructure.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that adapts training systems (e.g., Megatron-LM) for inference, Dual is architected ground-up for the latency-critical inference path, introducing a scheduler that treats the inference computation graph as a predictable, stateful process to optimize.

proposals.title: High-Throughput Inference for Non-Generative LLM Tasks: A Unified System for Embedding and Scoring
proposals.motivation: The recent focus on high-throughput generative inference for LLMs addresses a critical bottleneck in deployment. However, many real-world applications, such as retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), semantic search, and sequence scoring for reranking, rely heavily on non-generative tasks like embedding extraction and next-token probability scoring. These tasks are currently underserved by specialized high-throughput systems, leading to inefficient resource utilization and scalability challenges in production pipelines.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the core principles of continuous batching, memory optimization, and kernel fusion that enable high-throughput text generation can be systematically adapted and extended to create a unified, high-throughput inference engine for embedding generation and sequence scoring tasks, achieving order-of-magnitude throughput improvements over naive sequential execution.
proposals.method: We will extend the system architecture from 'High-throughput Generative Inference of Large Language Models with a Single GPU' to support non-generative workloads. The method involves: 1) Designing a new, unified computational graph representation for embedding (e.g., last token, mean pooling) and scoring (e.g., log-likelihood, perplexity) tasks using a relational abstraction, informed by prior work on Auto-Differentiation of Relational Computations. 2) Developing a dynamic batching scheduler that groups heterogeneous requests (generate, embed, score) based on their shared computational subgraphs (e.g., the forward pass through the base model) to maximize GPU utilization. 3) Implementing custom fused kernels for the final computation layers specific to embedding aggregation and scoring, minimizing memory I/O.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver an open-source, high-throughput inference system that generalizes beyond text generation, significantly accelerating the embedding and scoring workloads foundational to modern NLP pipelines. It will provide the first holistic evaluation of LLM inference systems across the full spectrum of core tasks, establishing new benchmarks for throughput and latency in non-generative settings.
proposals.novelty: While high-throughput batching is established for generation, its principled application to the structurally different problems of batched embedding extraction and sequence scoring is unexplored. The key novelty is a unified execution planner that optimizes across these heterogeneous task types within a single serving instance, a significant advance over task-specific serving solutions.

proposals.title: FlexGen-LS: A Low-Latency Offloading Engine for Interactive LLM Inference on a Single GPU
proposals.motivation: The FlexGen framework demonstrated high-throughput LLM inference via aggressive offloading, but its design prioritizes batch processing, leading to high latency unsuitable for interactive tasks. As LLMs are increasingly deployed in conversational agents and real-time applications, there is a critical need for efficient single-GPU inference that balances throughput with latency. This gap limits the accessibility of large models for latency-sensitive, on-demand use cases.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by redesigning FlexGen's scheduling and offloading strategies with a latency-first objective—focusing on prompt processing and token generation latency—we can achieve interactive response times for large models on a single GPU without sacrificing throughput efficiency.
proposals.method: We will develop a new latency-aware scheduler that dynamically manages the movement of model layers between GPU, CPU, and disk, prioritizing the prompt's critical path. This involves: 1) implementing a fine-grained, pre-fetching offloading strategy based on real-time generation state, 2) designing a novel chunking and pipelining mechanism for the forward pass to overlap computation and I/O more effectively for single sequences, and 3) integrating a lightweight performance model to adaptively choose optimal offloading plans based on prompt length and available memory. The evaluation will measure time-to-first-token and per-token latency under interactive workloads.
proposals.contribution: This work will extend the FlexGen system to support low-latency, interactive inference, making large-model deployment feasible for real-time applications on resource-constrained hardware. It will provide a systematic analysis of the throughput-latency trade-off in offloading-based inference and release an open-source engine that benchmarks against existing high-throughput and low-latency baselines.
proposals.novelty: While prior offloading work (like FlexGen and LLM.int8()) focuses on throughput or memory reduction, our approach is the first to co-design the offloading engine's core scheduler and dataflow specifically for minimizing latency of single-sequence generation, a non-trivial shift from batch-oriented optimization.

proposals.title: Federated Aggregation for Decentralized Multi-GPU Inference: Enabling Large Model Serving on Memory-Limited Devices
proposals.motivation: The deployment of large language models (LLMs) is constrained by the memory capacity of individual GPUs, making single-device inference infeasible for many users. While model parallelism and multi-GPU setups exist, they often require high-bandwidth, centralized hardware. There is a critical need for efficient, decentralized inference methods that can coordinate across heterogeneous, memory-limited devices, such as personal workstations or edge clusters, without centralized parameter storage.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that federated learning aggregation algorithms (e.g., FedAvg, FedProx), traditionally used for distributed training, can be effectively adapted to coordinate the forward-pass computations of a single LLM partitioned across multiple decentralized GPUs, enabling high-throughput inference without a central parameter server.
proposals.method: We will adapt the CorgiPile data shuffling and pipeline execution model to manage the flow of activation tensors between devices holding different model partitions. Inspired by 'Auto-Differentiation of Relational Computations', we will design a relational execution plan that treats devices as federated clients. The core technical innovation is replacing gradient aggregation with an activation aggregation step using methods like weighted averaging (FedAvg) or proximal regularization (FedProx) to combine partial outputs from different model segments, ensuring numerical stability and convergence of the multi-step inference process.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel systems framework for decentralized LLM inference, extending the federated learning paradigm beyond training. It will provide a formal analysis of the convergence rate and communication efficiency of federated aggregation during inference, and release an open-source prototype integrated with existing high-throughput inference codebases.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in repurposing federated aggregation—a training-centric technique—for the fundamentally different challenge of coordinated multi-device inference, creating a decentralized alternative to traditional model parallelism that does not assume a unified memory space or fast interconnect.

proposals.title: MegaBatch: Maximizing LLM Training Throughput on Memory-Abundant, Interconnect-Limited Multi-GPU Systems
proposals.motivation: As of 2025, large-scale LLM training is dominated by data-parallel strategies optimized for fast interconnects (e.g., NVLink, InfiniBand). However, many academic and industrial clusters feature nodes with plentiful GPU memory but relatively slow inter-node links (e.g., Ethernet). Existing frameworks underutilize this abundant memory, leaving potential throughput gains unexplored. This work inverts the typical scaling problem to address a common but under-optimized hardware configuration.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by co-designing a novel data-parallel training strategy with a memory-centric execution planner, we can significantly increase per-iteration throughput on memory-abundant, interconnect-limited systems by drastically reducing the frequency and volume of gradient synchronization.
proposals.method: We will develop a system that extends Yuan's prior work on Auto-Differentiation of Relational Computations and CorgiPile data management. The core is a 'MegaBatch' algorithm that loads a massive, sharded data batch into the aggregate GPU memory of a node. We will implement a delayed, compressed gradient synchronization protocol, where each GPU performs many local forward/backward passes on its shard before a single, communication-efficient all-reduce. The system will include an autotuner to optimize the local step count and batch sharding strategy based on a model of interconnect bandwidth and GPU memory capacity.
proposals.contribution: This will deliver a new, open-source training system module for high-throughput LLM pre-training and fine-tuning on commodity clusters. We will provide a theoretical analysis of the convergence rate under delayed synchronization with large local steps and empirically demonstrate a 2-5x throughput improvement over standard Distributed Data Parallel (DDP) on Ethernet-connected multi-GPU nodes for models of 7B to 30B parameters.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior large-batch or local-step methods focused on convergence, our work is a systems-first optimization for a specific, prevalent hardware constraint, tightly integrating data loading (CorgiPile), relational computation graphs, and a novel synchronization scheduler to exploit memory abundance as a substitute for fast interconnects.

proposals.title: CorgiPile++: A Single-GPU Training and Inference System for Trillion-Parameter Models via Hierarchical Offloading and Differentiable Relational Memory
proposals.motivation: As of 2025, scaling large language models (LLMs) to trillion parameters is hindered by prohibitive GPU memory requirements, forcing reliance on expensive multi-GPU/TPU clusters. Enabling single-GPU training and inference of such models would democratize access to frontier AI, reduce operational costs, and open new research avenues for personalization and on-device intelligence. This directly addresses the hardware bottleneck identified in prior work on high-throughput single-GPU inference.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by integrating a novel, auto-differentiable relational memory manager with a hierarchical, convergence-aware offloading scheduler, we can train and infer trillion-parameter models on a single GPU while maintaining a provable convergence rate comparable to full-parameter in-memory training.
proposals.method: We will extend the CorgiPile data management system into a unified memory hierarchy for model parameters, activations, and optimizer states. A differentiable relational computation engine (inspired by Auto-Differentiation of Relational Computations) will manage swapping between GPU HBM, CPU RAM, and NVMe, treating memory accesses as a relational optimization problem. The scheduler will use a learned model, trained via reinforcement learning on synthetic memory access graphs, to predict and prefetch critical parameter blocks based on training dynamics (gradient norms) and data shuffle order, minimizing the stall rate. Convergence analysis will formally bound the error introduced by aggressive offloading.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) an open-source system for single-GPU trillion-parameter model experimentation, 2) a new formal framework for analyzing the convergence of distributed, offload-based training algorithms under constrained memory, and 3) empirical benchmarks on scaled-up model architectures, providing a holistic evaluation of the quality-efficiency trade-off.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the co-design of a differentiable relational memory manager with a convergence-aware offloader, moving beyond static swapping or pipeline parallelism. This treats the entire memory hierarchy as a differentiable, trainable component of the learning system itself, which is non-obvious given existing static offloading or manual scheduling approaches.

proposals.title: Independent Subnet Training for Efficient Distributed Learning of Convolutional and Recurrent Networks
proposals.motivation: Distributed training of large models faces significant communication bottlenecks, especially for non-fully-connected architectures like CNNs and RNNs which have complex, structured parameter dependencies. Prior work on Independent Subnet Training (IST) for fully-connected networks shows promise for high-throughput, communication-efficient training by isolating and training random subnetworks, but its applicability to dominant vision and sequence models remains unexplored. Extending IST to these architectures could enable more scalable and resource-efficient training for a broader class of models.
proposals.hypothesis: The core principles of IST—randomly masking parameters to create independent, trainable subnets—can be effectively adapted to the weight-sharing structures and spatial/temporal dependencies of CNNs and RNNs, achieving competitive convergence rates and final accuracy while drastically reducing communication overhead in distributed settings.
proposals.method: We will design novel parameter masking strategies tailored to convolutional filters and recurrent cells, preserving local connectivity patterns essential for feature extraction. For CNNs, we will explore filter-wise and channel-wise masking within layers. For RNNs, we will investigate masking connections within gated cells (e.g., LSTM, GRU) while maintaining temporal unfolding. We will integrate these strategies into a distributed training framework, leveraging asynchronous or periodic model averaging (inspired by CorgiPile and recent shuffle-based methods) to combine independently trained subnet updates, and rigorously analyze the convergence properties.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic framework for applying IST to CNNs and RNNs, expanding the scope of communication-efficient distributed training. It will deliver new algorithms, a theoretical analysis of convergence for these architectures, and empirical benchmarks on standard datasets (e.g., ImageNet, WikiText) demonstrating trade-offs between accuracy, training throughput, and communication cost.
proposals.novelty: While IST has been studied for simple MLPs, adapting it to architectures with weight sharing and structured dependencies is non-trivial and requires novel masking designs. This proposal directly bridges the gap between efficient distributed systems methods and the core architectures of modern deep learning.

proposals.title: Fed-IST: Iterative Shuffling for Efficient and Private Federated Learning with Non-IID Data
proposals.motivation: Federated learning (FL) faces significant challenges with non-IID client data, which harms model convergence and final accuracy. While client sampling and data shuffling can mitigate this, directly shuffling raw data across clients is prohibited due to core privacy constraints. A method is needed that provides the convergence benefits of data shuffling without compromising the privacy guarantees of the FL framework.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying the Iterative Shuffling Technique (IST), which decouples data access from model updates via a trusted shuffler, to the FL setting will provide provable convergence benefits for non-IID data while enhancing privacy beyond standard secure aggregation.
proposals.method: We will adapt the IST architecture for cross-silo FL, treating each client's local dataset as a private shard. A central, trusted shuffler will coordinate a multi-round protocol: in each round, it receives client model updates (e.g., gradients), applies a permutation, and forwards the anonymized, shuffled updates to an aggregator for averaging. We will analyze the convergence rate of this Fed-IST protocol under standard non-IID assumptions and implement it using a distributed systems framework, evaluating its performance on benchmark tasks from the Holistic Evaluation of Language Models suite.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel FL algorithm with a formal analysis of its convergence properties under non-IID data. It will provide a practical, system-level implementation demonstrating improved accuracy-privacy trade-offs compared to FedAvg and methods using only secure aggregation.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the specific integration of IST's shuffling primitive into the FL loop to address non-IID convergence, a distinct approach from prior work on local steps or regularization. It leverages shuffling for both statistical efficiency and as an additional privacy layer.

proposals.title: IST-MP: Iterative Shuffling Training for Extreme Model Parallelism in HPC Clusters
proposals.motivation: Training models with trillions of parameters requires extreme model parallelism, which is bottlenecked by expensive and synchronous communication (e.g., pipeline bubbles, all-reduce). Pre-2026, methods like Megatron-LM and DeepSpeed's 3D parallelism still face significant idle time and memory constraints at the scale of thousands of devices. A new training paradigm is needed to efficiently utilize HPC clusters for next-generation models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying the Iterative Shuffling Training (IST) framework—which decouples data sampling from gradient computation via asynchronous, shuffled data delivery—to extreme model parallelism can drastically reduce device idle time and improve statistical efficiency, enabling faster convergence for massive models on thousands of devices.
proposals.method: We will extend the IST framework, building on Yuan's prior work on CorgiPile and auto-differentiation of relational computations. The core method involves: 1) Designing a hierarchical shuffling buffer system that feeds data asynchronously to different pipeline stages across nodes, minimizing bubbles. 2) Integrating IST's convergence guarantees with model-parallel gradient computation (e.g., tensor and pipeline parallelism), ensuring the full distributed training rate is maintained. 3) Implementing a prototype using a system like PyTorch with custom communication backends, evaluated on clusters with thousands of GPUs, measuring throughput and convergence against baselines like Megatron-LM.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, scalable training system (IST-MP) that demonstrates superior throughput and convergence for multi-trillion parameter models on large-scale HPC infrastructure. It will provide both theoretical convergence analysis for IST under model-parallel constraints and open-source system-level optimizations for very large scale machine learning.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fusion of IST's asynchronous, shuffle-based data management with extreme model parallelism, a combination not explored in pre-2026 literature. This moves beyond simply scaling existing parallel strategies to co-designing the data pipeline and model parallelism for holistic efficiency.

proposals.title: Dual-Phase Model Compression via Iterative Sparse Training and Subnet Consolidation
proposals.motivation: Deploying large language models (LLMs) on resource-constrained devices is a major challenge. Post-training pruning and distillation are common, but often lead to significant accuracy loss or require extensive re-training. A more efficient method is needed to directly extract a high-performing, compact model from a dense pre-trained network without prolonged fine-tuning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying Iterative Sparse Training (IST) to a pre-trained dense model can efficiently identify a high-performing sparse subnet, and that this subnet can be consolidated into a dense, compact final model with minimal performance degradation, outperforming standard one-shot pruning.
proposals.method: We will first apply an IST algorithm (e.g., RigL or SET) to a pre-trained LLM, using a dynamic sparse mask that evolves during a short, distributed fine-tuning phase to find a performant sparse architecture. Second, we will extract the weights and connectivity of the final sparse subnet. Finally, we will 'densify' this architecture by creating a new, smaller dense model initialized with these weights, followed by a brief convergence phase using a high-throughput, shuffle-based data pipeline to recover any lost accuracy.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel two-stage pipeline for model compression that decouples architecture search from final model creation. It will provide a systematic framework for generating ready-to-deploy compact models from large pre-trained checkpoints with higher accuracy than conventional pruning. The resulting tools will integrate with auto-differentiation systems for efficient large-scale training.
proposals.novelty: Unlike typical IST which trains a sparse model from scratch, we use IST as a post-training architecture search tool on a pre-trained model. The key novelty is the 'dual-phase' approach: using the discovered sparse subnet as a blueprint to construct a final dense, compact model, rather than deploying the sparse model itself.

proposals.title: IST-DP: A Hybrid Parallel Training Framework for Large Models Combining In-Node Sequence Tensor Parallelism with Cross-Node Data Parallelism
proposals.motivation: Training modern large language models (LLMs) requires sophisticated parallelism strategies to fit models into GPU memory and scale across clusters. Existing approaches like standard pipeline or tensor parallelism introduce significant communication overhead or complex synchronization. A hybrid strategy that efficiently partitions work within and across nodes is needed to maximize hardware utilization and training throughput for models with hundreds of billions of parameters.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that combining In-Node Sequence Tensor (IST) parallelism, which partitions sequence dimensions and model tensors within a multi-GPU node, with traditional Data Parallelism (DP) across nodes will yield superior training throughput and convergence rates compared to homogeneous parallelism strategies, by optimizing intra-node communication efficiency and inter-node scalability.
proposals.method: We will design and implement a hybrid parallel framework. Within a node, we employ IST, as explored in prior work on high-throughput inference, to partition the sequence length and model weights across GPUs, leveraging high-bandwidth NVLink. Across nodes, we use standard data parallelism with synchronous gradient aggregation (e.g., via All-Reduce) and gradient sharding. We will develop a unified scheduler to manage the two parallelism dimensions, implement efficient communication primitives, and integrate the approach with a compiler-based auto-differentiation system (like in 'Auto-Differentiation of Relational Computations') to handle the complex computational graph. The training convergence and throughput will be evaluated on large transformer models using datasets from the 'corgipile' corpus.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new, production-ready hybrid parallel training framework for LLMs. It will provide a detailed performance model and empirical analysis comparing IST-DP to pure DP, pipeline parallelism, and 3D tensor parallelism. The resulting system will demonstrate improved training efficiency on large clusters, advancing the state-of-the-art in distributed machine learning systems.
proposals.novelty: While data and model parallelism are well-known, the specific integration of the intra-node IST strategy—originally designed for inference—with cross-node data parallelism for training is novel. This hybrid approach uniquely targets the hardware hierarchy of modern clusters to minimize the most expensive communication costs.

proposals.title: HetTrain: A System for Heterogeneous, Distributed Training with Adaptive Architecture and Precision
proposals.motivation: The standard paradigm for distributed training of large models assumes homogeneous architectures and uniform precision (e.g., FP16/BF16) across all devices, leading to inefficiencies. This ignores the heterogeneous nature of real-world hardware clusters (e.g., mixing high-end and older GPUs, CPUs with accelerators) and the varying computational sensitivity of different model components. A system that can exploit this heterogeneity is crucial for cost-effective and scalable training, especially for massive models where hardware homogeneity is a bottleneck.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by relaxing the homogeneous parameter assumption and allowing different model parts (e.g., attention heads, MLP blocks) to be trained with different architectures (e.g., sparse vs. dense) or numerical precisions across a heterogeneous device cluster, we can achieve comparable model quality with significantly improved training throughput and resource utilization.
proposals.method: We will develop HetTrain, a distributed training system building on Yuan's prior work on Auto-Differentiation of Relational Computations. The core is a dynamic partitioning and scheduling algorithm that profiles device capabilities and model layer sensitivities. It will map computational subgraphs to optimal devices using mixed-precision strategies (FP32, BF16, FP8) and optionally substitute specific layers with more efficient variants (e.g., using structured sparsity or alternative activation functions). A novel gradient synchronization protocol, extending ideas from CORGiPile's data shuffling, will ensure convergence despite architectural and precision heterogeneity across nodes.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel system, HetTrain, for heterogeneous model training, 2) a formal analysis of the convergence rate under such heterogeneous conditions, extending prior homogeneous analyses, and 3) empirical benchmarks on large language models demonstrating significant throughput gains on heterogeneous clusters without sacrificing final task performance.
proposals.novelty: While prior work explores mixed-precision or conditional computation, HetTrain is the first to co-optimize device heterogeneity, layer-wise architectural variation, and precision in a single, end-to-end trainable system, moving beyond static, uniform model graphs.

proposals.title: AsyncPipe: Asynchronous Pipeline Parallelism for Geo-Distributed Large Model Training
proposals.motivation: Training modern large language models (LLMs) requires massive compute clusters, often forcing co-location in data centers to enable low-latency synchronous communication. This limits access to geographically distributed, heterogeneous resources (e.g., specialized accelerators across institutions). Synchronous model-parallel methods like PipeDream suffer severe pipeline bubbles and idle time under high-latency WAN links, drastically reducing training throughput and hardware utilization.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by relaxing the strict synchronous step assumption in pipeline parallelism and introducing a carefully bounded asynchronous parameter update scheme, we can achieve stable convergence and significantly higher throughput for LLM training across geo-distributed, high-latency devices.
proposals.method: We will design AsyncPipe, a system extending pipeline parallelism (e.g., GPipe) with an asynchronous, event-driven execution engine. Micro-batches will be processed as soon as device dependencies are met, not on a global clock. To manage staleness, we will implement a versioned weight-tracking mechanism and a dynamic gradient clipping rule based on per-parameter update age, informed by prior work on asynchronous SGD convergence rates. We will integrate this with a compiler-based auto-differentiation framework (inspired by Auto-Differentiation of Relational Computations) to manage the complex dependency graph and perform static analysis for optimal micro-batch scheduling and buffer allocation.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel asynchronous pipeline-parallel training algorithm with bounded staleness guarantees, 2) an open-source system implementation enabling efficient use of geo-distributed clusters, and 3) an empirical evaluation on large-scale models (e.g., GPT-3 architecture variants) demonstrating maintained model quality with significantly reduced training time under simulated and real WAN conditions compared to synchronous baselines.
proposals.novelty: While asynchronous data-parallel SGD is well-studied, applying asynchrony to the model-parallel dimension, particularly for the sequential dependencies in transformer-based pipeline training, is largely unexplored. Our approach uniquely combines pipeline scheduling with formal convergence analysis tailored for high-latency links.

proposals.title: CorgiPile-Inference: Distributed Collaborative Inference of Large Language Models on Heterogeneous Edge Devices
proposals.motivation: The deployment of large language models (LLMs) for latency-sensitive applications on resource-constrained edge devices remains a critical challenge. Centralized cloud inference introduces privacy risks and network latency, while existing model compression techniques often sacrifice capability. A collaborative, distributed inference paradigm that pools the memory and compute of many edge devices could enable powerful, private, and responsive LLM services without requiring any single device to host the full model.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the tasklet-based computational abstraction and scheduling framework from CorgiPile, proven for distributed training, can be effectively adapted to orchestrate the distributed, layer-wise execution of a single LLM inference request across a dynamic pool of heterogeneous edge devices, achieving predictable latency and high system throughput.
proposals.method: We will extend the CorgiPile framework to treat each layer or block of a transformer-based LLM as a schedulable 'inference tasklet'. A central coordinator will partition the model graph, profile device capabilities (compute, memory, network), and allocate tasklets using a cost model minimizing end-to-end latency. We will implement a pipelined execution protocol with efficient activation caching and communication, leveraging pre-2026 techniques like activation quantization and peer-to-peer RDMA (where available) to reduce overhead. The system will be evaluated using real-world edge device clusters and models like LLaMA-7B/13B.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel systems design and open-source prototype for collaborative LLM inference on edge networks, 2) a formalization of the inference tasklet scheduling problem with heterogeneity and reliability constraints, and 3) an empirical analysis of the latency-throughput trade-offs and failure resilience of this decentralized approach compared to cloud and on-device baselines.
proposals.novelty: While federated learning and split inference exist, applying a fine-grained, dynamic tasklet scheduler—originally designed for large-scale *training* on homogeneous clusters—to the *inference* of a single request across a volatile, heterogeneous edge network is a novel systems optimization challenge with distinct constraints.

proposals.title: Sparsity-Aware Kernel Fusion and Quantization for High-Throughput LLM Inference
proposals.motivation: The deployment of large language models (LLMs) is bottlenecked by memory bandwidth and compute intensity, especially on resource-constrained devices. Pre-2026, individual optimizations like quantization, kernel fusion, and dynamic sparsity have shown promise, but their interactions are poorly understood and often implemented in isolation. A holistic, co-designed system is needed to unlock compounded, real-world speedups for generative inference.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by co-designing contextual sparsity patterns (e.g., from methods like CorgiPile) with quantization-aware kernel fusion, we can achieve super-additive performance gains and higher throughput for LLM inference on a single GPU, surpassing the sum of applying each technique independently.
proposals.method: We will develop a compiler-based system that first profiles and induces structured, context-aware sparsity in transformer FFN layers. This sparsity pattern will then inform a mixed-precision quantization scheme (e.g., INT8/FP16), applied selectively to dense and sparse components. Finally, we will design and generate fused GPU kernels that natively execute the sparse, quantized matrix operations, minimizing data movement and kernel launch overhead. The system will be evaluated using holistic benchmarks on modern LLM architectures.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a unified inference engine that demonstrates the synergistic potential of combined sparsity, quantization, and fusion. It will provide a systematic framework for co-optimizing these techniques and release open-source kernels to push the frontier of single-GPU LLM throughput, directly extending research on high-throughput generative inference.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies not in the individual techniques, but in their tight integration: designing sparsity patterns that are optimal for subsequent quantization and hardware-aware kernel fusion, moving beyond the layer-wise application of independent optimizations common in pre-2026 work.

proposals.title: CorgiPile-Train: Exploiting Contextual Activation Sparsity for High-Throughput Distributed Training of Large Language Models
proposals.motivation: Training large language models (LLMs) is prohibitively expensive, dominated by dense matrix multiplications in attention and feed-forward layers. However, recent pre-2026 analyses, like those in holistic model evaluations, show that token-level activations exhibit significant, context-dependent sparsity. Current distributed training systems, while optimized for hardware, treat these computations as uniformly dense, wasting FLOPs and communication bandwidth on negligible values.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by dynamically identifying and exploiting structured, contextual sparsity patterns in activations during forward and backward passes, we can design a distributed training system that maintains model quality while significantly improving throughput and reducing communication costs.
proposals.method: We will extend the CorgiPile data management philosophy to training, building a system that profiles activation sparsity patterns offline on a diverse corpus. This profile will inform a runtime scheduler that, for each transformer block, dynamically masks negligible activations and their corresponding gradients. We will integrate this with efficient, sparse collective communication primitives (inspired by auto-differentiated relational computations) and a convergence-guaranteed optimizer that accounts for the masked updates. The training algorithm will be implemented and evaluated using a full-stack, distributed framework like usepackage.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel distributed training system that reduces computational and communication overhead via dynamic sparsity, 2) a rigorous empirical analysis of the convergence rate and final model quality when using such methods, and 3) open-source system components that integrate sparsity-aware scheduling with data shuffling and pipeline parallelism.
proposals.novelty: While inference-time sparsity is well-studied, applying contextual sparsity dynamically to the training loop—especially the backward pass and gradient synchronization in a distributed setting—is largely unexplored. Our method's novelty lies in co-designing the sparsity pattern discovery with the distributed training system's data and communication layers.

proposals.title: CorgiPlan: A Holistic Optimizer for Large-Scale Machine Learning Compute Plans in Relational Databases
proposals.motivation: The integration of machine learning (ML) training and inference with relational databases (RDBMS) is critical for scalable data science, as seen in systems like CorgiPile. However, the compute plans generated for very large-scale ML workloads, which involve complex relational computations and iterative gradient updates, are not optimized holistically by traditional query planners. This leads to inefficient resource utilization and slow convergence, especially in distributed settings with data shuffling and full-batch operations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a novel, holistic optimizer that jointly reasons about relational algebra, iterative model updates, and distributed system constraints can significantly improve the end-to-end execution time and convergence rate of large ML compute plans within an RDBMS.
proposals.method: We will develop CorgiPlan, an optimizer extending the RDBMS query planner. It will ingest a compute plan comprising relational preprocessing (joins, filters) and an iterative ML training loop. CorgiPlan will use a cost model incorporating data shuffling costs, convergence dynamics, and GPU memory constraints. Key techniques include: 1) automatically differentiating relational computations to fuse gradient steps with data transformations, 2) introducing novel plan rewrites that interleave data sampling (shuffle) with model updates to optimize for convergence rate, and 3) generating a distributed execution schedule that minimizes communication for full-batch or large-minibatch operations.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new optimization framework and a set of rewrite rules specifically designed for ML workloads inside an RDBMS. It will provide a system for end-to-end optimization of data preparation and model training, leading to demonstrable improvements in throughput and time-to-accuracy. The resulting open-source prototype will advance the field of database systems for ML.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that optimizes relational queries or distributed ML training in isolation, CorgiPlan's novelty lies in its holistic optimization across the boundary of declarative SQL and imperative training loops, using convergence-aware cost models to guide physical plan selection.

proposals.title: CorgiPile++: Benchmarking DBMS-Optimized Out-of-Core Training for Trillion-Parameter Models
proposals.motivation: The scaling of large language models has surpassed the aggregate RAM capacity of even large GPU clusters, making out-of-core training a critical necessity. While database management systems (DBMS) offer proven techniques for managing data larger than memory (e.g., buffer pools, indexing, prefetching), their application to the unique access patterns of massive model training—characterized by shuffled, sequential reads of model states and gradients—remains underexplored and unquantified.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a DBMS, architected with ML-specific optimizations (e.g., for the CorgiPile data layout), can demonstrably outperform traditional file-system-based out-of-core methods in training throughput and convergence rate for models exceeding aggregate RAM, by more efficiently managing I/O and memory hierarchy.
proposals.method: We will extend the CorgiPile data structure and its associated shuffle algorithms to operate within a modified DBMS kernel (e.g., based on DuckDB or a custom lightweight engine). We will implement and benchmark a distributed, out-of-core SGD variant where model shards and optimizer states are stored as relational tables, leveraging DBMS buffer management and query planning for efficient block-wise swapping between SSD and GPU RAM. The convergence rate and throughput will be empirically tested against baseline file-system implementations (e.g., using memory-mapped files) on synthetic workloads and large model architectures (e.g., scaling up known models like GPT-3) under fixed memory constraints.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first rigorous, apples-to-apples benchmark of DBMS-driven versus OS-driven out-of-core training for massive models. It will yield a set of design principles and an open-source prototype (CorgiPile++) that demonstrates how database techniques can be specialized for ML systems, directly addressing a key bottleneck in post-2025 model scaling.
proposals.novelty: While DBMS-for-ML is not new, prior work focuses on data preprocessing. The novelty lies in applying and quantifying core DBMS optimizations (transactional buffer management, query optimization) specifically to the management of the model parameters themselves during distributed training, a shift from managing training *data* to managing the *model* as data.

proposals.title: CORGITune: Reinforcement Learning for Auto-Tuning Distributed Machine Learning Query Plans
proposals.motivation: The execution of large-scale machine learning workloads, such as those described in 'Auto-Differentiation of Relational Computations', involves complex, multi-stage compute plans (queries) over massive datasets like CORGIPile. Manually tuning these plans for optimal resource utilization and convergence rate on distributed systems is intractable. Pre-2026, automated query optimization lacks adaptive, workload-aware methods that can learn from system feedback to minimize end-to-end training time.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a reinforcement learning agent, trained on a rich state representation of the distributed compute graph and system telemetry, can learn a policy to automatically tune plan parameters (e.g., operator placement, shuffle strategy, batch size) that outperforms static heuristics and accelerates the convergence of the overall ML training job.
proposals.method: We will build upon the relational auto-differentiation framework to instrument compute plans, extracting a state vector of operator costs, data dependencies, and cluster resource metrics. We will design a deep Q-network (DQN) agent that takes this state as input and outputs tuning actions (e.g., changing the degree of parallelism for a shuffle). The agent will be trained via online interaction with a cluster simulator and validated on real distributed training jobs, using job completion time and convergence rate as the reward signal.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel RL-based auto-tuner integrated with a large-scale ML computation graph compiler, 2) a public benchmark of tunable distributed ML plans derived from CORGIPile workloads, and 3) empirical evidence showing reduced time-to-accuracy for several model architectures compared to baseline optimizers.
proposals.novelty: While RL has been applied to database query optimization, its application to the unique domain of very large-scale, iterative ML training plans—where the objective is not just latency but statistical convergence—is novel. This bridges the gap between systems-for-ML and learning-for-systems research in a concrete way.

proposals.title: Dual: Declarative, Optimized Inference for Large Models via Relational Query Compilation
proposals.motivation: The computational cost of large model inference is a critical bottleneck, especially for batched or multi-step queries over diverse datasets. Current systems treat inference as an imperative, low-level procedure, missing opportunities for cross-request optimization, data-aware scheduling, and seamless integration with data preprocessing. A declarative approach could unlock systematic optimizations akin to those in database systems, improving throughput and resource utilization.
proposals.hypothesis: By representing model inference as a declarative query plan over a relational abstraction of input data, we can apply database-style optimization techniques (e.g., predicate pushdown, join reordering, common subexpression elimination) to significantly improve inference throughput and latency in distributed, data-intensive settings.
proposals.method: We will develop a system, Dual, that extends relational algebra operators to include a `MODEL_APPLY` operator encapsulating a model forward pass. Using the relational computation and auto-differentiation expertise from prior work (e.g., Auto-Differentiation of Relational Computations), we will build a query compiler that translates a high-level inference specification into an optimized, distributed execution plan. This plan will fuse data preprocessing (filtering, joining) with model computation, leverage shuffling strategies for optimal data locality, and apply cost-based optimization using pre-profiled model and data statistics to maximize hardware utilization, particularly on GPU clusters.
proposals.contribution: 1) A novel framework that unifies data management and model inference under a declarative paradigm. 2) A set of optimization techniques tailored for inference workloads, demonstrating measurable gains in throughput and cost-efficiency over imperative baselines like vLLM or standard PyTorch serving. 3) A publicly available system prototype that enables complex, optimized inference pipelines over very large datasets.
proposals.novelty: While prior work optimizes inference kernels or scheduling in isolation, Dual is the first to treat the entire inference workload—from raw data to model outputs—as a single, optimizable declarative query, applying full-database optimization theory to the ML inference domain.

proposals.title: CorgiPile++: High-Intensity, In-Memory Training via Differentiable Relational Shuffling and Kernel Fusion
proposals.motivation: The ML community's focus on scaling to massive, distributed datasets has overshadowed the potential of maximizing computational intensity on smaller, high-quality datasets that fit in memory. Many real-world applications, especially in enterprise and scientific domains, are constrained by data availability, not compute nodes. Pre-2026, systems like CorgiPile and methods for auto-differentiation of relational computations have shown the value of smart data ordering and operator fusion, but they have not been fully leveraged for compute-bound, single-node training.
proposals.hypothesis: By co-designing a relational data shuffling system with a kernel fusion compiler for complex ML operations, we can achieve faster convergence and higher effective FLOP/s utilization on a single GPU or multi-core CPU, outperforming standard batch training on the same modest-sized dataset.
proposals.method: We will extend the CorgiPile framework to generate not just static data orders, but dynamic, learnable shuffling policies expressed as relational algebra plans, made differentiable via prior work on auto-differentiation of relational computations. These policies will be optimized jointly with the model to maximize training signal per epoch. Concurrently, we will implement a compiler pass that fuses sequences of high-overhead operations (e.g., attention, normalization, specialized activations) into single, custom CUDA/CPU kernels, minimizing memory traffic. The training loop will integrate these components, using online metrics to adapt the shuffle strategy and kernel fusion graph.
proposals.contribution: 1) A new open-source system for compute-intensity-optimized training, demonstrating superior time-to-accuracy on in-memory datasets. 2) A formalization of 'differentiable shuffling' as a resource optimization problem. 3) Empirical evidence that for many tasks, investing in algorithmic and systems efficiency on a single device can be more effective than distributed data-parallel scaling.
proposals.novelty: This work uniquely combines the data-centric optimization of relational learning systems (CorgiPile) with the low-level compute optimization of kernel fusion, creating a holistic, single-node training accelerator, whereas prior art typically addresses these domains separately or focuses on distributed scaling.

proposals.title: EC-DLM: Error-Compensated and Debiased Local Momentum for Communication-Efficient Federated Learning
proposals.motivation: Communication bottlenecks remain a primary constraint in distributed and federated optimization for large-scale machine learning. While gradient compression and local momentum methods (like FedAvg) are common solutions, their naive combination suffers from biased updates and error accumulation, especially under data heterogeneity, leading to unstable convergence and degraded final accuracy. A principled integration is needed to achieve both high compression rates and robust performance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by decoupling the momentum state from the compression error state and applying a debiasing correction before the momentum update, we can design a local SGD variant that maintains the convergence rate of full-precision methods even under aggressive gradient compression and non-IID data distributions.
proposals.method: We will develop the EC-DLM algorithm, which maintains dual local states: model parameters and a compression error accumulator. In each iteration, the local stochastic gradient is first corrected by subtracting the stored error, creating an unbiased estimate. This corrected gradient updates a local momentum buffer, which then updates the model parameters. The difference between the corrected gradient and its compressed version becomes the new error for the next round. We will analyze its convergence theoretically for non-convex objectives and evaluate it empirically on benchmark vision and language tasks using realistic federated data partitions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, robust algorithm for communication-efficient distributed optimization with a formal convergence guarantee. It will deliver an end-to-end system-level evaluation demonstrating practical speedups in federated learning scenarios without sacrificing model accuracy, directly addressing the trade-off between compression and statistical efficiency.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior error-compensation methods applied after momentum or to plain SGD, EC-DLM's core novelty is the pre-momentum error feedback and the explicit decoupling of the two states, which fundamentally alters the interaction between compression noise and acceleration, preventing bias propagation.

proposals.title: Scaling Asynchronous-Conjugate Stochastic Gradient Descent: A Holistic Evaluation on Multi-Billion Parameter Models and Diverse Datasets
proposals.motivation: Asynchronous-Conjugate SGD (AC-SGD) has shown promise for efficient large-scale training, but its empirical validation remains limited to models under 1.5B parameters. With the field's push towards ever-larger models (e.g., GPT-3, PaLM) and the critical need for robust, data-efficient training algorithms, it is essential to rigorously test AC-SGD's scalability and generalization. This work addresses a key gap in understanding how asynchronous second-order-inspired methods behave in modern, massive-scale distributed learning environments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that AC-SGD will maintain its theoretical convergence benefits and superior training stability compared to standard first-order optimizers when scaled to models with 10B+ parameters, but its performance profile will be significantly influenced by dataset diversity and scale.
proposals.method: We will implement a distributed, sharded-data parallel version of AC-SGD, building on frameworks like PyTorch's FSDP. The evaluation will extend to models from 3B to 13B parameters (e.g., architectures similar to GPT-NeoX) and diverse datasets including The Pile, a multi-domain text corpus, and a curated image-text multimodal dataset from CorgiPile. We will measure key metrics: final loss, convergence rate, throughput, and memory efficiency, conducting a holistic comparison against AdamW and LAMB optimizers.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first large-scale empirical analysis of AC-SGD, establishing its practical limits and benefits for training frontier-scale models. It will deliver actionable insights on optimizer selection for massive-scale ML and contribute robust benchmarks to the systems-for-learning community.
proposals.novelty: While scaling studies are common, a systematic investigation focusing on the interaction between a novel asynchronous conjugate gradient method, model scale (beyond 1.5B), and dataset characteristics is novel. This bridges a specific gap between optimization theory and modern large-model training systems.

proposals.title: AC-SGD Beyond Pipelines: A Unified Framework for Asynchronous-Convergent Distributed Training
proposals.motivation: Asynchronous SGD (AC-SGD) has shown promise for pipeline parallelism by decoupling forward and backward passes to improve hardware utilization and throughput. However, modern large-scale training employs diverse paradigms like data parallelism with model sharding (e.g., Fully Sharded Data Parallelism), 3D parallelism, and hybrid schemes, each with unique synchronization bottlenecks. A principled extension of AC-SGD's core ideas to these paradigms could unlock significant efficiency gains across the distributed training stack, a critical need given the escalating cost of training frontier models.
proposals.hypothesis: The asynchronous-convergent principle of AC-SGD—tolerating bounded staleness in gradient updates to overlap computation—can be generalized into a pluggable coordination layer that maintains convergence guarantees while improving system throughput in non-pipeline parallel schemes like FSDP and tensor-model hybrid parallelism.
proposals.method: We will first formalize the AC-SGD convergence analysis for a generic dataflow graph of training operations, abstracting specific parallel schemes. Building on Yuan's expertise in Auto-Differentiation of Relational Computations, we will implement this as a middleware 'AC-Orchestrator' within PyTorch that intercepts and schedules collective operations (e.g., all-reduce, all-gather for FSDP) with controlled asynchrony. We will rigorously evaluate the method by integrating it into systems like FairScale/DeepSpeed, measuring throughput and convergence rate on benchmark models (e.g., GPT-3 architecture variants) trained on CorgiPile-style datasets, comparing against fully synchronous baselines.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver: 1) a theoretical framework extending AC-SGD convergence proofs to broader parallel topologies, 2) an open-source, practical system implementation for major non-pipeline paradigms, and 3) a holistic evaluation quantifying the trade-off between statistical efficiency and hardware utilization, providing clear guidelines for practitioners.
proposals.novelty: While AC-SGD was designed for linear pipelines, applying its core insight to the distinct communication patterns of FSDP and 3D parallelism is non-trivial and unexplored. The novelty lies in creating a unified abstraction that separates convergence-aware scheduling from parallel strategy specifics, moving beyond ad-hoc optimizations.

proposals.title: EdgeTrain: Communication-Efficient On-Device Training via Asynchronous Compensated SGD with Selective Gradient Shuffling
proposals.motivation: Training small models directly on edge devices (e.g., IoT sensors, smartphones) enables personalized, privacy-preserving learning without cloud dependency. However, existing distributed training methods like All-Reduce SGD are infeasible in such environments due to extremely low, variable bandwidth and frequent device dropout. Pre-2026, while AC-SGD (Asynchronous Compensated SGD) shows promise for straggler mitigation in data centers, its adaptation for the severe constraints of edge networks remains unexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a modified AC-SGD algorithm, integrated with a strategic local gradient shuffling mechanism and ultra-low-bit quantization, can achieve stable convergence for small models over unreliable edge networks, maintaining accuracy while reducing communication volume by over 99% compared to standard methods.
proposals.method: We will adapt the AC-SGD framework for edge networks by: 1) Designing a delayed gradient compensation scheme that operates with sporadic, peer-to-peer communication rather than a central parameter server, leveraging insights from Yuan's work on relational auto-differentiation for sparse updates. 2) Integrating a 'CorgiPile'-inspired selective gradient shuffling protocol, where devices periodically exchange a small subset of high-magnitude gradients to de-bias the asynchronous updates. 3) Employing aggressive (1-2 bit) error-compensated quantization for all communicated gradients, with local full-precision accumulation to preserve convergence rate guarantees.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, practical training system for heterogeneous edge device fleets, backed by a rigorous convergence analysis for non-convex objectives under extreme communication constraints. It will provide the first holistic evaluation framework for on-device training systems, measuring trade-offs between accuracy, communication cost, and energy consumption across real-world network traces.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the co-design of asynchronous compensation with selective in-network gradient shuffling for decentralized edge topologies, moving beyond data-center-focused AC-SGD. This addresses the core tension between communication frugality and convergence stability in a way prior federated or compressed SGD methods do not.

proposals.title: AC-3D: Asynchronous Convergence in 3D Hybrid Parallelism for Extreme-Scale Model Training
proposals.motivation: Training trillion-parameter models requires combining pipeline, tensor, and data parallelism (3D parallelism), but existing methods suffer from pipeline bubbles, synchronization overhead, and complex convergence dynamics. Pre-2026, frameworks like DeepSpeed and Megatron-LM have implemented these paradigms, but the theoretical and systems-level understanding of their joint optimization, especially for ensuring stable convergence, remains limited.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the Asynchronous-Convergent SGD (AC-SGD) framework can be formally extended and practically implemented to guarantee convergence in a unified 3D parallel setting, mitigating the inefficiencies of synchronous scheduling and enabling higher throughput for massive models without sacrificing statistical accuracy.
proposals.method: We will develop AC-3D by first formulating a unified model of gradient updates across pipeline stages (with asynchronous forward/backward passes), tensor-parallel device groups, and data-parallel workers. We will design a novel gradient buffering and synchronization protocol, inspired by AC-SGD's relaxed consistency model, to overlap communication and computation across all three dimensions. The implementation will be built upon and extend a system like CorgiPile, integrating efficient tensor partitioning (as in Yuan's auto-diff of relational computations) and a holistic evaluation framework to monitor training stability.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver: 1) a formal convergence guarantee for AC-SGD under 3D hybrid parallelism, 2) an open-source system implementation demonstrating higher hardware utilization and throughput compared to synchronous 3D baselines, and 3) empirical validation on a multi-trillion parameter model scale, providing a recipe for extreme-scale training.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the formal integration of asynchronous convergence theory with the full stack of 3D parallelism, moving beyond co-designing only two parallelism forms and explicitly tackling the compounded staleness and synchronization challenges unique to the combined paradigm.

proposals.title: SparseSync: A Relaxed Synchronization System for Efficient Distributed Training of Models with Structured Gradient Sparsity
proposals.motivation: The dominant paradigm for distributed training of large models, like LLMs, relies on dense, synchronous gradient aggregation (e.g., All-Reduce), which is communication-heavy and inefficient when updates are naturally sparse or structured. Pre-2026, methods like Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) and models with heavy embedding layers exhibit such sparsity, but systems are not optimized for it, creating a bottleneck. This inefficiency limits the scale and speed of training for next-generation architectures that do not require full-model updates every step.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by formally relaxing the strict, dense synchronization requirement and designing a system that dynamically identifies and communicates only significant, structured gradient updates, we can achieve near-linear scaling in distributed training for sparse-update models without compromising final model convergence or quality.
proposals.method: We will develop SparseSync, a system that integrates a lightweight, online sparsity profiler into the training loop (inspired by CorgiPile's data-aware scheduling) to detect structured gradient patterns (e.g., for expert layers or embedding tables). It will use a relaxed consistency model, propagating only updates above a dynamically tuned significance threshold. We will implement novel, sparse collective communication primitives atop frameworks like PyTorch, and rigorously analyze the convergence rate under this relaxed regime, extending prior theoretical work on asynchronous SGD to structured sparsity.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a new open-source system for efficient sparse-update training, 2) a theoretical analysis of convergence under our relaxed synchronization model, and 3) empirical validation on large-scale MoE and recommendation models, demonstrating significantly reduced communication overhead and faster time-to-accuracy compared to dense All-Reduce baselines.
proposals.novelty: While gradient sparsification exists, SparseSync uniquely focuses on *system-level relaxations* for *inherently and structured* sparse models (not artificially compressed dense gradients), co-designing the scheduling, communication, and consistency models holistically, which is a departure from prior point solutions.

proposals.title: Provable Adversarial-Robust Convergence for Distributed Training via Relaxed Synchronization and Adaptive Shuffling
proposals.motivation: Modern large-scale ML systems prioritize raw throughput, often using aggressive asynchrony and data sharding that can lead to non-convergence or poor solutions under adversarial conditions like stragglers, network partitions, or biased data shuffles. As models grow in cost and societal impact, guaranteed convergence becomes a critical requirement for reliable and trustworthy training, even at the expense of peak speed.
proposals.hypothesis: By designing a novel relaxation of distributed SGD that formally inverts the priority from speed to robustness, we can construct a training framework that provides provable convergence guarantees under a defined class of adversarial system conditions, without reverting to fully synchronous execution.
proposals.method: We will develop a 'Convergence-First Distributed SGD' (CF-DSGD) protocol. It integrates (1) a dynamically adjustable synchronization window based on online convergence diagnostics, (2) an adversarial-aware data shuffling algorithm for CorgiPile that prioritizes statistical stability over locality, and (3) a lightweight coordination mechanism using vector clocks to enforce a relaxed yet provable consistency model across workers. Convergence will be analyzed under a Byzantine-resilient framework adapted for realistic system failures.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new distributed training system with formal convergence proofs under adversarial conditions, a set of configurable relaxation knobs for robustness-speed trade-offs, and an empirical evaluation on large-scale tasks demonstrating recoverable convergence where standard high-throughput methods fail.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that adds robustness as an afterthought to fast systems, we co-design the learning algorithm and system architecture from the ground up with convergence guarantees as the primary constraint, creating a new point in the design space for reliable large-scale ML.

proposals.title: FedShuffle: Communication-Efficient Federated Learning with Non-IID Data via Adaptive Gradient Synchronization and Stratified Sampling
proposals.motivation: Federated learning (FL) systems must handle highly non-IID data distributions across clients, a common real-world scenario that severely degrades model convergence and accuracy. Existing communication strategies, designed for IID or mildly skewed data, are inefficient under extreme statistical heterogeneity, leading to excessive communication rounds and poor utilization of critical data from minority-class clients. This problem is central to scaling FL for practical applications like personalized healthcare or on-device language modeling.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an adaptive communication strategy, which dynamically prioritizes and stratifies client updates based on local gradient divergence and class distribution, can significantly improve the convergence rate and final accuracy of federated models trained on highly non-IID data.
proposals.method: We will develop FedShuffle, a system that integrates two core techniques: 1) an adaptive synchronization protocol that selectively aggregates gradients from clients exhibiting high loss or gradient dissimilarity, measured via a lightweight proxy, and 2) a stratified client sampling mechanism, inspired by CorgiPile's data shuffling principles, that ensures underrepresented data classes are prioritized in each training round. We will implement and evaluate this system using a distributed, full-batch training framework, analyzing convergence under controlled non-IID splits.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, system-aware algorithm for communication-efficient FL under extreme non-IID conditions, backed by rigorous convergence analysis.
