It will contribute a new open-source module for federated optimization systems and a holistic evaluation benchmark for non-IID FL scenarios, extending prior work on Auto-Differentiation of Relational Computations to federated settings.
proposals.novelty: While client sampling and adaptive aggregation exist, FedShuffle's novelty lies in its joint optimization of communication efficiency and statistical fairness via a stratified, gradient-aware protocol, directly tackling the convergence bottlenecks of prior methods in highly skewed data regimes.

proposals.title: RelaxedSync: System Relaxation Techniques for High-Throughput Single-Node Multi-GPU Training
proposals.motivation: As large language models (LLMs) grow, distributed training across many nodes is standard, but it introduces significant communication overhead and system complexity. Single-node multi-GPU servers, with high-bandwidth NVLink interconnects, offer a promising alternative for efficient medium-scale training, yet existing distributed optimization frameworks do not fully exploit this relaxed, homogeneous hardware environment. Adapting system-level relaxations for this scale can unlock higher hardware utilization and faster convergence for research teams and small organizations.
proposals.hypothesis: By adapting and co-designing system relaxation techniques—specifically relaxed synchronization, communication-computation overlap, and intelligent gradient shuffling—for the single-node multi-GPU setting, we can achieve near-linear scaling efficiency and faster time-to-convergence compared to standard distributed data-parallel baselines.
proposals.method: We will build upon the 'system relaxation' concepts from prior large-scale systems work (e.g., CorgiPile's data management) and adapt them for a single node. The core is a novel synchronization scheduler that leverages NVLink topology to implement partial, staggered all-reduce operations, relaxing barrier strictness. We will integrate this with an auto-differentiation-aware data pipeline (inspired by Auto-Differentiation of Relational Computations) to optimize gradient computation and communication overlap. The training loop will employ a controlled, in-memory shuffle of data chunks across GPUs to improve convergence rate, moving beyond simple random shuffling.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver 1) an open-source, lightweight PyTorch extension for optimized single-node multi-GPU training, 2) a formal analysis of convergence rates under our proposed relaxed synchronization model, and 3) empirical benchmarks on LLM fine-tuning tasks demonstrating superior throughput and time-to-accuracy compared to standard DistributedDataParallel and Fully Sharded Data Parallel (FSDP) within a single server.
proposals.novelty: While system relaxations are studied in large clusters, their principled application to the high-bandwidth, low-latency single-node domain is unexplored. The novelty lies in the co-design of topology-aware synchronization, in-memory data shuffling, and automatic differentiation to maximize the unique advantages of a homogeneous multi-GPU server.

proposals.title: CorgiPile-DC: A High-Throughput, Latency-Tolerant Data Pipeline for Cross-Data-Center Distributed Training
proposals.motivation: The push towards trillion-parameter models requires aggregating compute and data resources beyond a single cluster, necessitating training across geographically distributed data centers. Pre-2026, synchronous distributed training (e.g., All-Reduce) is severely bottlenecked by high inter-DC latency, while naive asynchronous methods suffer from convergence degradation. This work addresses the critical gap in efficient, scalable training protocols for this high-latency, high-bandwidth environment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by co-designing a globally shuffled, latency-aware data pipeline (CorgiPile) with a novel delayed-gradient synchronization protocol, we can achieve near-linear throughput scaling and maintain statistical convergence rates comparable to intra-cluster training, despite significant communication delays.
proposals.method: We will extend the CorgiPile data management system to operate across DCs, implementing a hierarchical shuffling strategy that maximizes local batch diversity while minimizing cross-DC transfers. For gradient synchronization, we will design and analyze a 'Staggered Parallel SGD' protocol, which pipelines gradient computation and communication using historical gradient buffers (inspired by asynchronous SGD stability analysis) to hide latency. Convergence will be formally analyzed under non-i.i.d. data partitions across DCs.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) an open-source, high-throughput cross-DC training system architecture, 2) a novel synchronization protocol with a proven convergence bound for high-latency environments, and 3) an empirical benchmark on large-scale tasks (e.g., LLM pretraining) demonstrating viable cross-DC scaling, providing a blueprint for extreme-scale model development.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work focused on intra-cluster optimization or simple model parallelism, our approach uniquely co-designs the data loading semantics (CorgiPile) with the synchronization algorithm, treating high latency as a first-class constraint rather than just a network overhead to minimize.

proposals.title: CorgiPile-D: A Dynamic Benchmarking Framework for Evaluating Continual Learning in Deployed Language Models
proposals.motivation: Current LLM evaluation relies on static benchmarks (e.g., MMLU, HELM), which fail to capture the dynamic nature of models that learn continuously from user interactions post-deployment. This creates a critical gap in understanding model drift, performance degradation, and adaptation in real-world systems, posing risks for reliability and safety. A new evaluation paradigm is needed to assess models that evolve over time.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a dynamic, user-interaction-simulated benchmark will reveal significant performance divergence and emergent failure modes in continually learning models that are not captured by static evaluations, challenging the sufficiency of current benchmarking practices.
proposals.method: We will develop CorgiPile-D, extending the CorgiPile data-centric framework to generate dynamic, temporally-evolving evaluation tasks. Using a distributed, shuffling data scheduler, we will simulate sequential user interactions that introduce new knowledge, contradictions, and distribution shifts. We will measure the convergence rate and stability of models under this stream, employing auto-differentiation of relational computations to trace how specific interactions influence model outputs over time, creating a holistic evaluation of dynamic adaptation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic framework for dynamic benchmarking of continually learning LLMs, enabling rigorous evaluation of long-term adaptation and drift. It will contribute a new, open-source tool (CorgiPile-D) to the community and offer empirical evidence on the limitations of static benchmarks for deployed AI systems.
proposals.novelty: This moves beyond static snapshot evaluation to a temporal, interactive benchmark, directly challenging a core assumption in LLM assessment. It uniquely combines data-centric pipeline engineering (CorgiPile) with simulation of continual learning dynamics, a gap in pre-2026 evaluation suites.

proposals.title: HELM-Stream: A Continuous and Distributed Benchmarking Framework for Evolving Language Models
proposals.motivation: The rapid, continuous release of new and updated language models creates a moving target for evaluation, making static benchmark snapshots quickly obsolete. This hinders the community's ability to track genuine progress, identify regressions, and understand longitudinal trends in model capabilities and biases. A systematic, automated, and ongoing evaluation regime is therefore critical for transparent and evidence-based advancement in the field.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that implementing a continuous, automated, and resource-efficient benchmarking pipeline will reveal significant non-monotonic progress in language model capabilities over time, with performance on specific tasks fluctuating based on training data and architectural shifts, rather than improving uniformly.
proposals.method: We will extend the HELM (Holistic Evaluation of Language Models) framework into a continuous service, HELM-Stream. Leveraging distributed systems expertise (e.g., CorgiPile, Auto-Differentiation of Relational Computations), we will design a scheduler that automatically pulls new model releases from major hubs (e.g., Hugging Face). The core technical challenge is efficient, high-throughput evaluation; we will adapt methods from 'High-throughput Generative Inference' to perform scalable, parallel benchmarking across a cluster, optimizing for the convergence of evaluation metrics across shuffled data subsets to ensure statistical reliability while managing computational cost.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first publicly available, continuously updated leaderboard and dataset of model performance trajectories, offering a dynamic view of NLP progress. It will contribute a scalable, open-source benchmarking system that lowers the barrier for comprehensive model assessment and establishes a new standard for longitudinal evaluation in ML.
proposals.novelty: While HELM provided a seminal static benchmark, HELM-Stream introduces the novel paradigm of continuous evaluation as a service. Its technical novelty lies in the synthesis of large-scale distributed systems for data processing with high-throughput inference techniques to solve the unique systems challenges of perpetual, full-scale model evaluation.

proposals.title: CorgiPile-Sec: A Coded Secure Computation Framework for Robust and Private Federated Training
proposals.motivation: Federated learning (FL) faces dual challenges of device heterogeneity causing stragglers and the need for strong privacy guarantees via secure multi-party computation (MPC). Existing solutions treat robustness (e.g., coded computing for aggregation) and privacy (MPC protocols) as separate layers, leading to inefficiency. A unified framework is needed where the core secure computation itself is robust to dropouts and delays, enabling reliable, private collaboration in highly dynamic, large-scale settings.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating polynomial coding schemes directly into the secret-shared state of an MPC protocol, we can create a federated training system that guarantees both information-theoretic privacy against curious participants and information-theoretic robustness to a bounded number of device dropouts or stragglers within a single computation round.
proposals.method: We will design CorgiPile-Sec by embedding Lagrange coded computing (LCC) into the SPDZ-family MPC protocol. Local data shards will be encoded into polynomials before secret sharing. Participants compute on coded secret shares, and the MPC's Beaver triple generation and multiplication will be adapted to operate on this encoded domain. The decoding of the final function output (e.g., a gradient) will be performed collaboratively within the MPC, requiring only a subset of participants to finish, thus tolerating stragglers at the computation layer, not just the communication layer.
proposals.contribution: 1) A novel system, CorgiPile-Sec, that unifies coded robustness and cryptographic privacy for FL. 2) A theoretical analysis proving convergence rates under bounded dropouts and a distributed, adversarial threat model. 3) An open-source implementation and holistic evaluation on large-scale ML tasks, measuring throughput and comparing privacy-utility trade-offs against stacked (MPC + aggregation coding) baselines.
proposals.novelty: This moves beyond using coding for straggler mitigation in aggregation to making the secure computation itself straggler-tolerant, a non-trivial fusion of information-theoretic and cryptographic primitives not addressed in pre-2026 FL or MPC literature.

proposals.title: CorgiSplit: A Context-Aware, Multi-Objective Adaptive Partitioning Framework for Heterogeneous Health IoT Inference
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of heterogeneous IoT devices in healthcare, from wearables to bedside monitors, creates a complex ecosystem for deploying machine learning models. Static model partitioning fails under dynamic network and device conditions, while existing adaptive systems often optimize for a single objective like latency, neglecting critical trade-offs with energy and reliability. A unified framework that jointly optimizes for these competing goals based on real-time context is essential for scalable and trustworthy distributed health analytics.
proposals.hypothesis: A context-aware decision engine that learns a personalized partitioning policy for each device class—jointly optimizing over static hardware profiles and dynamic system states—will achieve superior and more reliable Pareto-optimal performance in multi-objective inference (latency, energy, accuracy) compared to static or single-objective adaptive baselines.
proposals.method: We will develop CorgiSplit, building on principles from CorgiPile for data/system profiling and Auto-Differentiation of Relational Computations for gradient-based optimization of the partitioning decision graph. The core is a lightweight, on-device context encoder and a centralized decision engine trained via multi-objective reinforcement learning (MORL). The engine ingests static device profiles (computed via micro-benchmarks) and dynamic telemetry (network RTT, server load, battery) to output an optimal partition point. We will formulate the optimization using a contextual bandit with a composite reward function weighted by application-specific QoS requirements.
proposals.contribution: 1) A novel unified optimization framework for context-aware model partitioning in heterogeneous IoT. 2) An open-source benchmark and evaluation suite for multi-objective edge inference in health IoT scenarios. 3) Empirical validation on a real-world testbed showing significant improvements in Pareto efficiency over state-of-the-art adaptive systems like Neurosurgeon and SPINN.
proposals.novelty: This work moves beyond network- or latency-only adaptation by fusing static heterogeneity profiling with dynamic context for multi-objective optimization, creating *personalized* partitioning policies per device class—a synergy not explored in pre-2026 split computing or edge AI literature.

proposals.title: CorgiPile for Healthcare: A High-Throughput, Distributed Framework for Training Clinical Deep Learning Models on Real-World Datasets
proposals.motivation: The application of deep learning to real-world healthcare data, such as electronic health records (EHRs) and medical imaging, is hindered by the massive scale and complex relational structure of the data, which challenges existing training pipelines. Pre-2026, frameworks like CorgiPile have shown promise for efficient, distributed training on web-scale corpora, but their performance and convergence properties on sensitive, heterogeneous, and highly structured medical datasets remain unvalidated. This gap limits the translation of scalable systems research into clinically impactful models.
proposals.hypothesis: The CorgiPile framework, leveraging its auto-differentiation of relational computations and high-throughput data shuffling, can achieve superior convergence rates and final model performance compared to standard distributed training pipelines when applied to large-scale, real-world healthcare datasets and clinically relevant model architectures.
proposals.method: We will implement a rigorous empirical study using the MIMIC-IV EHR dataset and the CheXpert medical imaging dataset. We will adapt CorgiPile's relational data loader to handle the temporal and tabular structure of MIMIC-IV and the image-label mappings of CheXpert. We will benchmark training convergence and throughput for models including a Temporal Fusion Transformer (for EHR forecasting) and a DenseNet-121 (for chest X-ray classification), comparing against standard PyTorch DistributedDataParallel and TensorFlow pipelines. Key metrics will include time-to-accuracy, final AUC-ROC, and hardware utilization on a multi-GPU cluster.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic evaluation of a modern, web-scale training systems framework on real-world healthcare data, establishing concrete performance benchmarks. It will deliver open-source data loaders and configurations for MIMIC-IV and CheXpert within CorgiPile, lowering the barrier for scalable clinical ML research. The findings will guide the adaptation of future high-throughput training systems for the unique demands of the medical domain.
proposals.novelty: While distributed training is well-studied, the specific application and rigorous evaluation of a framework designed for web-crawled data (CorgiPile) to the structurally complex and privacy-sensitive healthcare domain is novel. The work uniquely bridges scalable systems research (CorgiPile, auto-differentiation of relational computations) with practical clinical ML, testing a non-obvious hypothesis about the transferability of data shuffling and pipelining optimizations.

proposals.title: AdaptiveSplit: Dynamic Neural Network Partitioning via Supply Chain-Inspired Online Optimization
proposals.motivation: The deployment of large neural networks on resource-constrained edge devices necessitates split computing, but static partitioning fails under fluctuating network and device conditions, leading to high latency and energy waste. Prior work on dynamic split points lacks a robust, continuously adaptive framework that holistically optimizes for both end-to-end latency and system-wide energy consumption. This gap limits the efficiency and reliability of distributed inference for applications like mobile AR and real-time language models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that formulating dynamic model partitioning as a continuous supply chain logistics optimization problem—where model layers are inventory and compute/network resources are transportation links—will yield an online algorithm that can outperform static and heuristic-based methods by minimizing a combined cost function under real-time environmental dynamics.
proposals.method: We will develop a lightweight online controller, inspired by logistics re-routing algorithms, that ingests real-time telemetry (device CPU/GPU load, battery state, network bandwidth/latency, server queue depth). It will maintain a continuously updated cost model, treating each potential partition point as a 'route' with associated latency and energy 'costs'. Using a distributed, event-triggered optimization loop (akin to methods in Yuan's prior work on Auto-Differentiation of Relational Computations), the controller will dynamically select the optimal split point to minimize a weighted objective. The system will be evaluated using a modified split version of a Transformer model under emulated and real-world mobile network traces.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel online optimization framework for adaptive split computing, grounded in supply chain analogies; 2) an open-source implementation and benchmark for dynamic partitioning under non-stationary conditions; 3) empirical evidence on the trade-offs between adaptation frequency, overhead, and gains in latency/energy for large model inference.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct conceptual and algorithmic translation of supply chain logistics optimization—a mature field with proven dynamic re-allocation strategies—to the split computing problem, moving beyond traditional static or threshold-based partitioning heuristics used in prior systems research.

proposals.title: Zeus: Adaptive Edge-Server Partitioning for Efficient Co-Training of Extreme-Scale Models
proposals.motivation: Training massive models (10B+ parameters) on distributed, heterogeneous edge data is crucial for personalized AI but is bottlenecked by device limitations and variable network conditions. Existing split learning and AC-SGD methods use static model partitions, leading to severe inefficiency under dynamic resource constraints and diverse client datasets, preventing their application to state-of-the-art model scales.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a dynamic, telemetry-driven algorithm that continuously optimizes the model partition point for the entire training loop (forward/backward/optimizer) can minimize total training time for extreme-scale models under real-world edge constraints, making AC-SGD viable for models previously considered too large.
proposals.method: We will develop a lightweight profiler to collect live telemetry (compute, memory, bandwidth, dataset stats). A decision engine, modeled as a constrained optimization problem, will use this data to periodically re-evaluate the partition point via a cost model predicting iteration time. We will integrate this into a modified AC-SGD framework, building upon distributed training systems like CorgiPile, and validate using large vision and language models on a simulated edge cluster.
proposals.contribution: 1) A novel framework for adaptive edge-server co-training of extreme-scale models. 2) An open-source system implementation demonstrating significant throughput gains over static partitioning baselines. 3) An analysis of the convergence properties and trade-offs of dynamic partitioning in heterogeneous federated settings.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior static split learning, our system dynamically adjusts the partition for the *entire training loop* based on live system and data telemetry, enabling AC-SGD for models an order of magnitude larger than previously studied in edge contexts.

proposals.title: AdaSplit: Adaptive Privacy Budget Allocation for Utility-Preserving Split Learning on Distributed Healthcare Data
proposals.motivation: Split learning enables collaborative training on sensitive healthcare data by splitting a neural network between a client (e.g., hospital) and a server. However, intermediate feature representations can leak private information, necessitating differential privacy (DP). A core challenge is that applying uniform DP noise severely degrades model utility for complex tasks like medical diagnosis. Pre-2026, methods lack a principled, data-aware strategy to allocate a limited privacy budget across the model's data flow to preserve task-critical features.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by profiling the task-relevant information content and privacy sensitivity of intermediate activations using a sanitized proxy dataset, we can formulate and solve an optimization problem to non-uniformly allocate the DP budget, thereby achieving a superior privacy-utility trade-off compared to static allocation baselines.
proposals.method: We will develop a system that first profiles client-side intermediate outputs using a small, public, sanitized proxy dataset to estimate per-layer sensitivity (via gradient norms) and mutual information with the label. We then formulate a constrained optimization problem to allocate the total privacy budget (epsilon) across layers, maximizing a utility proxy (e.g., mutual information preserved) subject to the DP guarantee. During training, we will implement adaptive Gaussian noise injection proportional to the allocated budget per layer and integrate it into a distributed, shuffling-based training pipeline to ensure convergence, drawing on expertise in large-scale ML systems.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a formal framework and optimization method for adaptive DP budget allocation in split learning, 2) an open-source system implementation for distributed healthcare ML that provides stronger utility under the same formal privacy guarantee, and 3) a holistic evaluation on real-world medical imaging and EHR benchmarks, quantifying resilience to membership and attribute inference attacks.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in moving beyond uniform or heuristic noise addition by using a data-driven, optimization-based allocation of the privacy budget across the model's computational graph, which is a non-obvious extension of standard DP-SGD and prior split learning privacy methods that treat all activations equally.

proposals.title: CorgiPile-Edge: A Holistic Framework for Efficient On-Device LLM Inference via Structured Pruning, Quantization, and Knowledge Distillation
proposals.motivation: The deployment of generative language models on resource-constrained IoT devices is hindered by their massive computational and memory footprint. While Yuan's prior work on CorgiPile and high-throughput single-GPU inference addresses server-side efficiency, a systematic approach for the extreme edge is lacking. Applying and co-optimizing established model compression techniques specifically for the IoT segment is critical to enabling low-latency, private, and ubiquitous intelligent applications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a synergistic application of structured pruning, aggressive quantization, and task-specific knowledge distillation, guided by a holistic evaluation framework, can reduce LLM size by over 10x while maintaining >90% of the accuracy on targeted IoT tasks, making on-device inference feasible.
proposals.method: We will build upon the CorgiPile data management system to create a compressed model training pipeline. First, we will apply structured magnitude pruning (e.g., block-wise pruning) to transformer layers. Second, we will implement post-training quantization (PTQ) to INT8 and explore quantization-aware training (QAT) for lower precision (e.g., INT4). Finally, we will use knowledge distillation, where a large teacher model (from the Holistic Evaluation suite) trains the pruned and quantized student, focusing on task-specific relational computations optimized via auto-differentiation techniques for efficient gradient flow.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) an open-source, end-to-end compression pipeline (CorgiPile-Edge) for producing IoT-ready LLMs, 2) a rigorous analysis of the accuracy-latency-memory trade-off frontier for compressed models on edge hardware, and 3) new benchmarks for on-device model performance within the holistic evaluation paradigm.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the holistic co-design and evaluation of multiple compression techniques specifically for the IoT deployment target, moving beyond isolated compression studies, and integrating this process directly into the scalable CorgiPile and auto-differentiation frameworks for efficient, large-scale compression training.

proposals.title: CAFO: Coded Adaptive Federated Optimization for Straggler-Resilient and Stable Training
proposals.motivation: Federated Learning (FL) faces two critical, often separately addressed challenges: slow convergence due to client data heterogeneity and training delays caused by straggling devices. While adaptive optimizers like Adam improve convergence, and coded computing mitigates stragglers, their naive combination fails because the server cannot compute correct adaptive moment updates until all raw gradients are decoded. This forces a trade-off between robustness and convergence rate.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by designing a novel coding scheme that embeds linearity properties compatible with Adam's update rule, a federated server can compute provably correct first and second-moment estimates directly from coded gradient blocks, enabling simultaneous straggler tolerance and accelerated, stable convergence.
proposals.method: We will design a Coded Adaptive Federated Optimization (CAFO) framework. Clients encode their local stochastic gradients using a systematic linear code (e.g., Reed-Solomon) and transmit coded blocks to the server. The server, upon receiving a decodable set of blocks, will perform two key operations: (1) decode the full-batch gradient sum, and (2) compute an unbiased estimate of the second-moment (uncentered variance) directly from the coded blocks using the linearity of the squaring operation for the code's arithmetic. This allows the immediate application of an Adam-like step. We will analyze the convergence rate under partial client participation and heterogeneous data.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new FL optimization algorithm that unifies straggler resilience and adaptive optimization, a theoretical convergence guarantee for this coded adaptive setting, and an open-source system implementation demonstrating significant wall-clock time reduction compared to FedAvg and coded non-adaptive baselines on benchmark tasks.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the mathematical design of gradient coding that preserves the algebraic structure needed for adaptive moment estimation, allowing meaningful optimizer steps before full client gradient recovery, which is not possible in prior coded FL or FedAdam work.

proposals.title: FedAdapt: Correcting Stale Moment Estimates for Adaptive Optimization in Heterogeneous Federated Learning
proposals.motivation: Federated learning (FL) on non-IID, sparse client data suffers from unstable convergence and degraded performance when using adaptive optimizers like Adam. This is because the server's global moment estimates become biased due to infrequent and statistically skewed client updates, a problem exacerbated in large-scale, heterogeneous deployments. Existing FL optimizers often sacrifice the benefits of per-parameter adaptive learning rates to ensure stability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by having clients maintain and transmit a lightweight history of recent local gradient contributions, the server can directly correct the staleness and statistical shift in its global moment estimates, enabling stable, high-performance adaptive optimization under extreme client sparsity and data heterogeneity.
proposals.method: We will design a client-aware adaptive optimizer where each client stores a rolling window of its past gradient updates. This local history is sent to the server with each parameter update. The server, using a modified Adam/LAMB algorithm, will compute a per-client correction factor based on the divergence between the client's historical trajectory and the current global state. This factor will be applied to correct the first and second moment estimates before updating the global model, ensuring the adaptive learning rates are computed from a de-biased signal.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new federated optimization algorithm that maintains the convergence rate benefits of adaptive methods in non-IID, sparse settings. It will provide both a theoretical analysis of the correction mechanism's impact on convergence and a scalable system implementation, validated on large-scale language model tasks using heterogeneous client data distributions.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior methods that clip, scale, or discard client updates to manage heterogeneity, our approach directly corrects the core adaptive optimization statistics using client-specific historical context. This integrates a form of lightweight temporal modeling into the optimizer itself, a novel direction for FL systems.

proposals.title: AdaServer: Adaptive Gradient Methods for Efficient Federated Optimization of Large Language Models
proposals.motivation: Federated learning and large-scale distributed training of LLMs face a critical bottleneck in server-side optimization when aggregating sparse, non-IID client updates. Standard synchronous SGD with momentum often suffers from slow convergence and instability in this setting, wasting computational resources. Adaptive gradient methods like Adam and LAMB have proven superior for dense, centralized training, but their effective integration into federated/server-side optimization with sparse updates remains an open systems challenge.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a carefully engineered, communication-efficient integration of the LAMB optimizer into the federated server's update rule will significantly improve convergence rate and final model quality compared to SGD-based methods, without imposing prohibitive overhead on the parameter server architecture.
proposals.method: We will design and implement AdaServer, a parameter server framework extension that maintains per-parameter first and second moment estimates for the global model. We will adapt the LAMB algorithm to operate on aggregated client gradients, incorporating layer-wise adaptive learning rate clipping and a novel compression scheme for the moment updates to minimize server-client communication. The system will be evaluated using the CorgiPile data workload simulator and benchmarked against standard SGD and FedAvg on holistic LLM evaluation tasks, measuring wall-clock time to target accuracy.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a production-ready, open-source integration of adaptive optimizers for federated LLM training, demonstrating measurable improvements in convergence speed and model performance. It will provide a systematic analysis of the trade-offs between optimizer sophistication, communication cost, and statistical efficiency in large-scale, sparse-update learning scenarios.
proposals.novelty: While adaptive methods are standard in centralized training, their efficient and effective deployment in a federated/server-side context—requiring careful handling of sparse updates, moment state management, and communication overhead—is a novel systems-for-ML contribution, bridging a gap between optimization theory and large-scale distributed training practice.

proposals.title: CorgiPile-Implant: Extreme Model Splitting for Asynchronous, Hardware-Aware Fine-Tuning of Foundational Models on Ultra-Low-Power Medical Implants
proposals.motivation: Implantable medical devices for chronic conditions (e.g., neural prosthetics, glucose monitors) generate rich, personalized physiological data, but current AI is static and cannot adapt post-deployment. Fine-tuning large foundational models directly on implants is impossible due to severe power, memory, and compute constraints. Existing federated learning approaches are insufficient as they assume clients can hold and train the full model, which is infeasible for implant-scale hardware.
proposals.hypothesis: By co-designing a novel, multi-level model splitting strategy with an asynchronous, hardware-aware communication protocol, we can enable meaningful, ultra-low-power fine-tuning of billion-parameter models where the implant only holds and updates a tiny, critical subset of parameters.
proposals.method: We will extend the AC-SGD convergence framework to a three-tier hierarchy (implant, wearable hub, cloud) with extreme, non-uniform model splitting. Inspired by CorgiPile's data- and computation-aware shuffling, we will develop a 'CorgiPile-Implant' scheduler that dynamically partitions model layers based on implant compute budget, data sensitivity, and real-time link quality. The implant will train its assigned sub-model via a compressed, error-feedback SGD variant, asynchronously pushing updates to the wearable hub, which orchestrates gradient aggregation and synchronization with the cloud-held model bulk.
proposals.contribution: 1) A novel algorithmic framework for extreme model splitting that formally guarantees convergence under severe asynchrony and communication constraints. 2) An open-source simulation testbed modeling implant hardware (e.g., ultra-low-power MCUs) and variable body-area network links. 3) A proof-of-concept for personalized, on-device learning for adaptive biomedical implants, moving beyond static inference.
proposals.novelty: This goes beyond standard federated learning by proposing a fine-grained, hardware-driven model splitting strategy where the client (implant) cannot ever host the full model, and the communication protocol is co-designed with the algorithmic convergence guarantees for ultra-low-power, unreliable networks.

proposals.title: GossipGradient: Decentralized Consensus Learning for Lifelong Adaptation on Non-Stationary Edge Streams
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of IoT and edge devices generates continuous, private, and non-stationary data streams, making centralized cloud-based learning impractical due to latency, bandwidth, and privacy constraints. Existing federated learning relies on a central coordinator, creating a single point of failure and bottleneck, while current decentralized methods lack robust mechanisms for collaborative adaptation to local and network-wide concept drifts in resource-constrained environments.
proposals.hypothesis: A fully decentralized system using randomized gossip protocols for model synchronization, coupled with local drift detection and peer-negotiated consensus on update relevance, can enable a network of edge devices to achieve stable, distributed online learning and adapt to concept drifts without any central server.
proposals.method: We will design a system where each device runs a lightweight online learner (e.g., a small neural network or linear model) on its local data stream. Devices will periodically initiate gossip exchanges with random peers to average model parameters, building on pre-2026 decentralized SGD convergence analyses. To handle drift, each device will employ a local statistical detector (e.g., based on Page-Hinkley or ADWIN). Upon detecting a potential local drift, a device will propose a 'drift consensus round' via gossiping, where peers evaluate the proposed update's utility on their own recent data, accepting it only if it improves local loss, thus forming a dynamic, data-informed consensus on network-wide adaptation.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel protocol for decentralized, data-dependent consensus formation for concept drift adaptation, 2) a theoretical analysis of the convergence and stability of gossip-based learning under non-IID, non-stationary streams, and 3) an open-source system implementation and evaluation on benchmarks simulating real-world edge data drifts.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior decentralized learning work focused on static data distributions, this integrates online learning, local drift detection, and a gossip-based negotiation mechanism to achieve collective adaptation, eliminating the central server entirely for lifelong learning at the edge.

ideas.title: Adaptive Partitioning for Extreme-Scale Edge-Server Co-Training
ideas.description: This research develops a framework for training extremely large neural networks (exceeding 10 billion parameters) using adaptive client-server partitioning. The core innovation is a dynamic algorithm that continuously optimizes the model split point not just for inference, but for the entire training loop (forward pass, backward pass, optimizer step), based on live telemetry of network bandwidth, device compute load, server cluster load, and the statistical properties of the diverse, on-device training datasets. This enables the scalable application of AC-SGD to models of unprecedented size for edge computing, as the system can automatically offload computationally intensive layers to the server or bring them on-device to minimize total training time while respecting resource constraints.

ideas.title: Error-Compensated and Debiased Local Momentum for Communication-Efficient Distributed Optimization
ideas.description: This research proposes a new algorithm, EC-DLM, that fundamentally redesigns how local momentum interacts with gradient compression. It integrates a dual-state mechanism: one state for the model parameters and another for tracking compression errors. Before applying momentum, the local gradient is corrected by the previous compression error, generating an unbiased estimate. This corrected gradient then updates both the local momentum buffer and the model, while the new compression error is stored for the next iteration. The system is evaluated end-to-end across heterogeneous data distributions and non-convex neural network tasks, demonstrating robust convergence matching full-precision baselines while achieving high compression rates.

ideas.title: Ultra-Low-Power In-Implant Fine-Tuning of Foundational Models via Extreme Model Splitting
ideas.description: This research direction investigates the extreme scaling of Asynchronous Communication SGD (AC-SGD) to enable the fine-tuning of foundational models with billions of parameters on ultra-low-power implantable devices. It requires developing novel, multi-level model splitting strategies that go beyond traditional federated learning to distribute massive model components across the implant, a local wearable hub, and the cloud, under severe and variable communication constraints. The core challenge is to co-design the algorithmic framework and the hardware-aware communication protocol to allow the implant's minimal compute unit to contribute meaningfully to learning from its unique, real-time physiological data stream. Success would enable a new class of smart, adaptive implants that personalize large-scale AI for chronic condition management without compromising patient safety or device longevity.

ideas.title: Client-Aware Adaptive Optimization with Staleness Correction for Federated Learning
ideas.description: This redesigned system integrates a novel adaptive optimizer for federated learning that directly addresses the bias in moment estimates caused by sparse, non-IID client updates. It requires each participating client to maintain a lightweight local history of its recent gradient contributions, which is sent alongside the current update. The server uses this history to apply a correction factor to its global Adam/LAMB moments, accounting for the staleness and statistical shift of each client's parameters. This approach ensures stable convergence and maintains the benefits of adaptive learning rates, even under extreme client sparsity and data heterogeneity.

ideas.title: Coded Adaptive Federated Optimization (CAFO)
ideas.description: This approach integrates coded computing principles with adaptive optimization methods like Adam at the server. The server receives coded gradient blocks from clients, which provide redundancy against stragglers. Crucially, the server can compute the necessary first and second-moment estimates (e.g., for Adam) directly on these coded representations, performing a meaningful optimization step as soon as a decodable set of blocks arrives, without waiting for all clients. This creates a system where straggler tolerance and faster, more stable convergence are achieved simultaneously, rather than treating them as separate problems.

ideas.title: Coded Secure Multi-Party Computation for Robust and Private Federated Collaboration
ideas.description: This research direction integrates coded computing principles directly into a secure multi-party computation (MPC) framework for federated learning. Instead of merely adding redundancy to model updates, the core computation—the privacy-preserving function evaluation on the raw, distributed data—is encoded across participants. This allows the collaborative task to complete correctly and privately even if a subset of devices disconnects or is excessively slow, as the encoded computation provides information-theoretic redundancy. The synergy creates a system that is both robust to dynamic participation and secure against curious participants or servers, moving beyond straggler mitigation in aggregation to straggler-tolerant secure computation.

ideas.title: Context-Aware, Multi-Objective Adaptive Partitioning for Heterogeneous Health IoT
ideas.description: This research develops a unified optimization framework that dynamically selects the optimal model partition point by jointly considering the static computational/memory profiles of heterogeneous devices and the dynamic state of the network, server load, and application-specific QoS requirements (e.g., latency for critical alerts, energy efficiency for wearables). The core synergy is a context-aware decision engine that learns and adapts a personalized partitioning policy for each device class, ensuring that a resource-constrained wearable and a powerful bedside monitor operate with different, yet simultaneously optimal, split configurations under fluctuating conditions. This enables scalable, efficient, and reliable distributed inference across diverse healthcare IoT ecosystems, which neither static adaptation to heterogeneity nor dynamic optimization based solely on network conditions could achieve alone.

ideas.title: Decentralized Collective Adaptation for Lifelong Edge Streams
ideas.description: This research designs a fully decentralized learning system for a network of resource-constrained edge devices, each processing its own continuous, non-stationary data stream. The core challenge is enabling online learning and concept drift adaptation through purely peer-to-peer communication, eliminating the central server entirely. Devices must collaboratively detect, negotiate, and adapt to local and network-wide concept drifts by forming dynamic consensus on model updates and drift boundaries through gossiping protocols. This results in a resilient, self-organizing learning fabric that continuously evolves from distributed data streams, achieving robust lifelong learning at the network edge.

ideas.title: Dynamic Model Partitioning via Adaptive Resource Allocation Inspired by Supply Chain Logistics
ideas.description: This research adapts solutions from supply chain logistics to the problem of dynamically choosing the optimal split point for a split neural network between device and server. In logistics, systems dynamically re-route shipments and re-allocate inventory between central hubs and local depots based on real-time data like traffic, warehouse load, and demand to optimize for cost and time. The analogous solution involves continuous optimization algorithms fed by sensor and operational data. Adapted to the computing context, we propose developing an algorithm that treats model layers as inventory and uses a continuously updated cost model. This model ingests real-time conditions—network bandwidth, latency, server load, and device computational capacity—to dynamically select the partition point that minimizes a combined objective of end-to-end latency and system energy consumption, effectively 're-routing' the computational pipeline in response to changing environmental 'traffic'.

ideas.title: Adaptive Privacy Budget Allocation for Utility-Preserving Split Learning in Healthcare
ideas.description: This system enhances split learning with differential privacy by intelligently distributing the privacy budget across the model's data flow. It first profiles the information content and sensitivity of the client's intermediate outputs using a small, sanitized proxy dataset. During training, it applies a non-uniform noise injection strategy, rigorously protecting identifiable features while preserving features essential for the server's learning task. This approach ensures a practical balance, maintaining strong formal privacy guarantees against inference attacks without rendering the collaborative model ineffective for its intended diagnostic or predictive healthcare purpose.

ideas.title: Adaptive Gradient Compression via Congestion Control Protocols
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by drawing an analogy from computer networking's TCP congestion control protocols. The analogous problem is managing data flow over a heterogeneous, shared network to maximize throughput without causing collapse, solved by algorithms like AIMD that dynamically adjust the transmission window. Adapted to AC-SGD, the research would develop a protocol where worker nodes continuously monitor the round-trip time and success rate of their gradient transmissions to a parameter server. Based on this, they would proactively adjust their compression ratios using an AIMD-like mechanism: additively decreasing compression (sending more precise gradients) when the network is responsive, and multiplicatively increasing compression (sending highly compressed/sparsified gradients) upon detecting signs of congestion or slow links. This creates a distributed, real-time optimization for heterogeneous network conditions that balances communication efficiency with learning stability.

ideas.title: Adapting Climate Model Downscaling Protocols for Healthcare AI Validation
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by drawing an analogy from climate science. The analogous problem is validating coarse global climate models, which is solved by 'downscaling' them to regional levels using high-resolution real-world observational data to test predictive accuracy for specific phenomena. Adapting this solution, we propose treating the AI framework as the 'global model' and validating it by 'downscaling' to specific, real-world clinical environments and tasks. This involves applying the framework to diverse, actual medical datasets (e.g., MIMIC-IV, CheXpert, institutional EHRs) and benchmarking it against clinically relevant deep learning models for concrete tasks like mortality prediction or image diagnosis. The goal is to rigorously test the framework's performance, generalizability, and robustness in the complex, heterogeneous 'climate' of real healthcare data, moving decisively beyond initial controlled studies.

ideas.title: Scale BAGUA to larger clusters beyond 128 GPUs

ideas.title: Preference-Aware, Multi-Objective Adaptive Partitioning for Edge-Cloud Systems
ideas.description: This research develops a system that dynamically selects a model partitioning strategy by balancing multiple competing objectives—latency, energy, privacy, and cost—according to real-time user context and implicit preferences. Instead of a single optimal split, it maintains a learned Pareto frontier of viable partition points and their trade-offs. A lightweight online learning module observes user interactions and system context to infer the current relative importance of each objective, selecting the frontier point that maximizes the contextual multi-attribute utility. This approach ensures robustness to diverse and shifting user priorities, making the adaptation truly user-centric rather than merely network-responsive.

ideas.title: Apply Secure Multi-Party Computation from Electronic Voting to Federated Learning Aggregation
ideas.description: This idea expands the original secure aggregation concept by drawing an analogy from electronic voting systems. In that field, protocols for homomorphic tallying solve the parallel problem of computing an aggregate (a vote count) from private inputs (individual ballots) without revealing any single input. The analogous solution uses cryptographic primitives that allow operations like addition on encrypted data. Adapted to the federated learning context, this means designing a protocol where client devices send their model updates in an encrypted form that supports homomorphic addition. The server can then sum all encrypted updates and decrypt only the final aggregate, thereby learning the necessary averaged update for the global model while provably preserving the confidentiality of each device's local data contribution. This provides a stronger, information-theoretic privacy guarantee compared to methods that rely on trust or differential noise added after decryption.

ideas.title: Collaborative Computation via a Cryptographic Immune System
ideas.description: This idea inverts the data isolation assumption by proposing a direct but intrinsically safe sharing mechanism, inspired by immunology. In vaccinology, the immune system is trained using attenuated or subunit versions of a pathogen that contain only the necessary antigens, avoiding exposure to the dangerous whole. Analogously, for device collaboration, we explore generating and sharing 'cryptographic antigens'—task-specific, non-invertible data representations (e.g., using feature extraction combined with differential privacy or one-way function ensembles). These representations would allow devices to compute collaboratively on the precise features needed for a task (like federated learning updates or joint inference) without exposing raw, isolated data, enabling a more efficient and direct form of privacy-preserving computation.

ideas.title: Adaptive Immune System for Continuous Device Streams
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution from computational immunology—specifically, the dual-layer, lifelong adaptive strategy of the biological immune system—to the problem of scaling continuous lifelong learning on non-stationary device streams. The analogous solution uses an innate (fast, general) and adaptive (slow, specific, memory-equipped) layer that generates diversity to detect new threats. Adapted to machine learning, the system features a static base model for common data patterns and a plastic model that maintains a core memory of past concepts. It proactively generates a population of varied model variants, evaluates them on incoming data to detect drift, and selectively updates the active model, enabling efficient online learning and robust concept drift adaptation for pervasive computing devices.

ideas.title: Budgeted Dual-Memory Lifelong Learning with Meta-Control for Non-Stationary Streams
ideas.description: This system addresses catastrophic forgetting and unbounded growth in continuous learning by implementing a budget-aware, dual-memory architecture. A stable core model preserves long-term knowledge, while a plastic auxiliary model handles recent concept drift, both updated online. A meta-controller, optimized via online reinforcement learning, arbitrates between stability and plasticity by allocating data, managing memory, and triggering consolidation events under strict computational and storage constraints. This ensures robust adaptation to non-stationary streams while guaranteeing the retention of critical foundational concepts and maintaining system viability on resource-constrained devices.

ideas.title: Convergence-Guaranteed Federated Training with Predictive Synchronization for Extended Disconnection
ideas.description: This system ensures robust, decentralized learning by preventing model divergence during server disconnection. Each device runs a local training regimen constrained by a dynamic, predicted envelope of the global model's evolution, calculated from recent update patterns. Devices also participate in opportunistic peer-to-peer consensus rounds when available to align their local directions. Upon reconnection, the server employs a provably convergent merging algorithm that integrates long-disconnected updates without requiring rollbacks or causing instability.

ideas.title: Apply signal compression techniques from seismology to IoT model compression
ideas.description: This idea adapts lossy signal compression techniques from seismology to the problem of model compression for IoT edge AI. In seismology, continuous, high-volume waveform data is compressed by preserving only the most information-rich frequency bands and transient events (like earthquakes), discarding noisy background data. Analogously, we can treat a neural network's parameters and activations as a complex signal. The adapted research idea is to develop a model compression framework that analyzes the 'information density' of parameters—identifying and preserving those critical for task performance (the 'seismic events') while aggressively pruning, quantizing, or even discarding redundant or low-sensitivity parameters (the 'background noise'). This approach, inspired by geophysical data reduction, could enable higher compression ratios than standard magnitude-based pruning for IoT devices, as it prioritizes the informational contribution of each parameter to the model's output.

ideas.title: Clinical-Edge Co-Design for Real-World Healthcare AI
ideas.description: This research direction proposes a clinical-edge co-design framework that integrates model compression techniques (pruning, quantization, distillation) directly into the validation pipeline on real-world, multi-modal healthcare datasets (e.g., EHR time-series, medical images). The core innovation is to tailor the compression process by analyzing the sensitivity of clinically critical model outputs (e.g., diagnostic accuracy for specific sub-populations, uncertainty in edge cases) to different compression methods, creating a feedback loop. This ensures the final lightweight model deployed on IoT devices maintains necessary clinical fidelity, moving beyond generic accuracy/FLOPs trade-offs to a principled, evidence-based compression for medical AI.

ideas.title: Adaptive Server-Side Optimization via Satellite Constellation Clock Synchronization Principles
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from distributed satellite systems, where a ground station must synchronize clocks across a constellation using sparse, noisy signals from individual satellites. The analogous solution uses adaptive filtering (e.g., Kalman filtering) that maintains a dynamic model of each satellite's clock drift, weighting incoming updates based on estimated reliability. Adapted to federated learning, the server would integrate adaptive gradient methods (like Adam or LAMB) not just on a global gradient stream, but by maintaining and updating per-client adaptive moment estimates (first and second moments) on the server side. These client-specific statistics are updated only when that client participates in a round. The server then uses these persistent, adaptive statistics to weight and combine the sparse client gradient updates, dynamically adjusting the effective learning rate and direction for each client's contribution. This should improve convergence and stability when client participation is highly irregular and gradient updates are sparse and heterogeneous, mimicking how ground stations achieve robust synchronization from intermittent satellite signals.

ideas.title: Adapting Phylogenetic Comparative Methods for Non-IID Healthcare Data
ideas.description: This idea addresses severe non-IID data across personal healthcare devices by drawing an analogy from evolutionary biology. In phylogenetics, trait data across species are not independent and identically distributed due to shared ancestry, solved using Phylogenetic Generalized Least Squares (PGLS) that models data covariance via a phylogenetic tree. Adapted to federated learning for healthcare, we propose constructing a 'health state similarity graph' for devices/users from metadata (diagnoses, demographics, device type) to explicitly model the expected statistical dependencies between their local data distributions. This graph-based covariance structure is then incorporated into the federated learning framework—for instance, by regularizing model updates based on relational proximity or using it to structure client clustering and personalized model initialization—thereby formally relaxing the homogeneous data distribution assumption and improving learning from uniquely heterogeneous health data sources.

ideas.title: Decentralized Secure Aggregation via Multi-Party Computation on Constrained Devices
ideas.description: This research direction synthesizes secure aggregation with a serverless architecture by designing a lightweight multi-party computation (MPC) protocol that runs directly on edge devices. The protocol allows a dynamic committee of peers to collaboratively compute the sum of encrypted model updates, with the final aggregated result revealed only when a sufficient threshold of participants agree. This eliminates the central server as a single point of failure and a privacy bottleneck, distributing the trust and computational load across the participating devices themselves. The core challenge is to engineer this cryptographic primitive to be efficient enough for the compute, memory, and communication constraints of typical IoT or mobile hardware.

ideas.title: Heterogeneity-Aware, Robustness-Constrained Model Compression for Dynamic IoT Environments
ideas.description: This research designs a system that moves beyond applying generic compression. It begins with a detailed profiling phase to capture the specific hardware capabilities and environmental context of a target IoT device class. Compression techniques (pruning, quantization, distillation) are then co-optimized under explicit constraints for model robustness, measured through adversarial and natural distribution shift testing. The final compressed model is deployed with a lightweight monitor that can trigger a fallback to a safer, pre-agreed operating point if runtime performance degrades, ensuring reliability in dynamic real-world conditions.

ideas.title: Causal Scaling Analysis: Isolating Architectural and Data Factors for AC-SGD Efficacy in Large Models
ideas.description: This research moves beyond mere scaling tests to perform a principled investigation into the conditions required for AC-SGD's success. It employs controlled experiments across multiple large model families (e.g., dense transformers, mixture-of-experts) and diverse, well-characterized datasets to disentangle the effects of architecture, data distribution, and optimization hyperparameters. The goal is to produce a predictive model or set of clear design principles that specify when and why AC-SGD outperforms standard optimizers at scale, transforming a validation effort into a foundational understanding of the method's limits and applicability.

ideas.title: Decentralized, Cache-and-Carry Learning Inspired by Epidemic Routing
ideas.description: This research adapts solutions from Delay-Tolerant Networking (DTN), specifically epidemic routing protocols, to the challenge of intermittent server connectivity in federated learning. In DTN, nodes store, carry, and opportunistically forward message copies to other nodes, enabling communication without a persistent connection. The adapted idea develops a 'cache-and-carry' learning protocol where devices continue local training during server disconnection and, upon encountering other devices, perform peer-to-peer model update exchanges. This creates a decentralized, epidemic propagation of learning progress through the network, ensuring robustness and continued model improvement even during prolonged central server unavailability, with eventual synchronization once a stable server link is re-established.

ideas.title: Adapting Distributed Sensor Network Protocols for Implantable Device Federations
ideas.description: This idea adapts solutions from distributed environmental sensor networks to the challenge of ultra-low-power implantable devices. In environmental science, networks of soil or climate sensors conserve energy by using hierarchical, event-driven protocols where leaf nodes transmit minimal processed data only to local aggregators. We adapt this by proposing a 'federation' of implants within a body, where secondary micro-implants perform extreme model splitting to run only tiny detection models. They communicate sparse, event-triggered signals to a designated primary implant aggregator, which has slightly more resources to fuse data and run a larger model segment. This structure minimizes the energy-intensive external communication to only the aggregator, pushing the scaling dual (computation vs. communication) to a finer, intra-body network level.

ideas.title: Adaptive Scaling of AC-SGD via Ecological Succession Principles
ideas.description: This idea proposes to solve the challenge of extending AC-SGD evaluation to significantly larger models and diverse datasets by drawing an analogy from ecological succession studies. In ecology, researchers use chronosequences of sites at different developmental stages to understand long-term ecosystem scaling without observing the entire timeline. The adapted solution is to create a 'model chronosequence' for AC-SGD: a systematic evaluation framework across a progressively scaled series of model sizes (from 100M to 7B+ parameters) and dataset diversities. By measuring key optimization indicators (e.g., loss landscapes, gradient variance, generalization gaps) across this sequence, we can model the 'successional trajectory' of AC-SGD. This will allow us to predict its stability, efficiency, and performance in the 10B+ parameter regime and on highly diverse data, thereby de-risking and informing the design of the final, large-scale experiments.

ideas.title: A Distributed Edge-Computing Mesh Inspired by Mycorrhizal Networks
ideas.description: This research explores scenarios where excess device capacity is pooled to perform server-like functions for other, more constrained nodes, inspired by the analogous problem of resource distribution in forest ecosystems. The analogous solution from forest ecology is the mycorrhizal network ('Wood Wide Web'), where fungi facilitate symbiotic resource sharing between plants, increasing overall system resilience. Adapting this, the research idea is to design a distributed edge-computing mesh protocol. This protocol would enable devices to autonomously form a peer-to-peer network, where resource-abundant nodes dynamically discover and allocate their excess computation, storage, and bandwidth to support nearby constrained nodes, governed by lightweight, symbiotic incentive and trust mechanisms, thereby scaling system capability through decentralized, organic coordination.

ideas.title: Adapting Interstellar Radio Telescope Synchronization for Cross-Institutional Federated Learning
ideas.description: This idea scales federated learning to cross-institutional hospital federations by drawing an analogy from Very Long Baseline Interferometry (VLBI) in radio astronomy. In VLBI, independent telescopes sync via timestamps and shared parameters to form a virtual telescope. Similarly, each hospital or institutional cluster acts as a powerful, autonomous server. They train a shared model architecture locally for agreed-upon intervals. A central coordinating entity, acting as the 'correlator,' periodically merges these updates using secure, verifiable timestamps and model hashes—mirroring how VLBI uses atomic clocks and observation logs. This creates a robust, scalable federation that minimizes continuous network dependency and enhances privacy, as raw data or granular gradients never leave the local institutional firewalls, only periodic, aligned model updates do.

ideas.title: Decentralized Swarm Computation via Stigmergic Coordination
ideas.description: This research challenges the assumption that a powerful central server is necessary for coordinating computation among constrained devices. By drawing an analogy from entomology and swarm intelligence, we adapt the solution of stigmergic coordination used by social insects. In the analogous biological system, agents indirectly coordinate by modifying their shared environment (e.g., laying pheromone trails), leading to emergent, robust global problem-solving without any central controller. We adapt this to design a fully decentralized computational system where constrained devices (IoT sensors, edge nodes) operate peer-to-peer. Devices coordinate by interacting with a lightweight, distributed ledger (the digital analog of the shared environment). Each device follows simple, pre-programmed rules triggered by specific states or transactions on this ledger. For example, a device completing a computation task writes a result to the ledger, which automatically triggers the next device in a workflow to begin its processing. This stigmergic approach allows complex, multi-step computations and data aggregation tasks to be performed efficiently across the swarm, with the ledger providing fault-tolerant coordination and state persistence, entirely eliminating the need for a central server.

ideas.title: Byzantine-Robust, Accountable Multi-Party Computation for Adversarial Collaboration
ideas.description: This system extends secure multi-party computation to withstand actively malicious participants who may deviate from protocols or submit corrupted data. It integrates cryptographic proofs to ensure participants follow the computation correctly and validate their inputs against predefined constraints. The architecture enables detection and exclusion of malicious parties during the computation while preserving privacy for honest participants. This creates a practical collaboration framework for real-world scenarios where some parties cannot be trusted to follow protocols faithfully.

ideas.title: Incentive-Aligned & Verifiable Device Resource Exchange Networks
ideas.description: This research investigates architectures for pooling excess device capacity where participation is driven by a cryptographically enforced incentive model, not altruism. Devices contribute compute, storage, or bandwidth in exchange for verifiable credits redeemable for equivalent resources from the pool later, creating a sustainable barter economy. The design prioritizes security through hardware-backed enclaves for task isolation and zero-knowledge proofs to ensure computational integrity without exposing private data. The outcome is a practical framework where the 'inverse' computation model becomes viable because it directly addresses the fundamental economic and security barriers to voluntary device participation.

ideas.title: Multi-Layer Privacy-Preserving Aggregation with Post-Aggregation Sanitization
ideas.description: This system enhances secure aggregation by adding local differential privacy at the client level to perturb individual updates before encryption. The server performs the cryptographic secure aggregation within a trusted execution environment (TEE), which then executes a post-aggregation privacy filter. This filter applies techniques like gradient clipping and the addition of minimal, calibrated noise to the decrypted aggregate, providing formal differential privacy guarantees for the final model update and mitigating inference attacks on the aggregated result.

ideas.title: Decentralized, Byzantine-Robust Federation for Cross-Institutional Hospital Networks
ideas.description: This system implements federated learning across hospital networks using a fully decentralized architecture where each hospital server participates as an equal peer. Coordination and secure model aggregation are managed via a permissioned blockchain or a practical Byzantine Fault Tolerant consensus mechanism, eliminating any central point of failure. The design ensures robustness even if a subset of hospitals are compromised or act maliciously, maintaining system integrity. This provides a more resilient and trust-minimizing foundation for scaling to large, real-world healthcare federations.

ideas.title: Context-Aware Reconstruction for Bit-Starved Implantable Sensing
ideas.description: This system addresses the fatal flaw of information loss in ultra-low-power implants by co-designing the on-implant encoder and external base station processing. The implant transmits only a minimal, maximally informative representation of sensed data, optimized for energy and bandwidth. The external unit employs a patient-adaptive probabilistic model that fuses this sparse data with historical context and physiological priors to reconstruct a high-fidelity, continuous data stream. This enables clinically reliable monitoring and decision-making from transmissions that would otherwise be too sparse or corrupted for direct use.

ideas.title: Sybil-Resilient Decentralized Computation via Web-of-Trust Attestation for Constrained Devices
ideas.description: This system achieves robust, fully decentralized computation among constrained devices by directly mitigating the Sybil attack vulnerability. It employs a decentralized web-of-trust where new nodes must be vouched for by multiple existing nodes with established reputations, earned through a history of completing verifiable useful work. Consensus and task assignment are weighted by this dynamically updated reputation score, preventing a sudden influx of malicious nodes from controlling the network. The design eliminates the central server while maintaining security through a trust layer that emerges from the devices' own proven contributions.

ideas.description: This adversarially improved idea moves beyond retrospective dataset testing to a prospective validation framework. It deploys and evaluates clinically relevant models on live, de-identified EHR data streams from multiple, geographically distinct healthcare institutions, ensuring temporal and external validity. The system rigorously stress-tests model performance against real-world challenges like distributional shift, missing data, and workflow integration. Finally, it incorporates comprehensive algorithmic fairness audits across protected subpopulations, measured against pre-specified benchmarks for clinical utility and safety.
